topics.title: Human Brain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT522 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Brain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the Human Brain

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Human Brain** is the central organ of the human nervous system, composed of approximately **86 billion neurons** ($\approx 86 \times 10^9$) and an even larger number of glial cells ($\approx 85 \times 10^9$). It serves as the biological inspiration and computational benchmark for **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)**. From a KTU 2024 perspective, the human brain is studied to extract computational principles — such as parallel distributed processing, fault tolerance, and learning via synaptic plasticity — that can be mathematically abstracted into artificial neuron models like the **McCulloch–Pitts (MCP) neuron** and the **Perceptron**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> The human brain is introduced as the *biological precursor* to artificial neural networks. The emphasis is on understanding how **biological neurons** receive, integrate, and transmit electrochemical signals, which forms the foundation for deriving mathematical neuron models later in the module.

### 1.2 Brain at a Glance — Key Physiological Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Adult Human) |
|---|---|
| Mass | $\approx 1.35$ kg (≈ 2 % of body mass) |
| Neuron count | $\approx 86 \times 10^9$ |
| Glial cell count | $\approx 85 \times 10^9$ |
| Synapses (average per neuron) | $\approx 7{,}000$ |
| Total synapses | $\approx 10^{14}$ to $10^{15}$ |
| Power consumption | $\approx 20$ W |
| Operating frequency (neuron firing) | $1$ – $200$ Hz |
| Resting membrane potential | $\approx -70$ mV |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Brain as a Gigantic Electrical City**
> Imagine a city of **86 billion buildings** (neurons), each connected to thousands of neighbours by **electrical wires** (axons) and **mail-receiving antennas** (dendrites). When a building "fires," it sends a tiny voltage pulse down its wires. The neighbouring buildings collect these pulses, add them up, and if the total is large enough, they fire too. The strength of the connections (synapses) keeps changing based on how often they are used — the more a connection is used, the stronger it gets. This is essentially **learning**. The artificial neural network is a tiny, simplified mathematical mimic of this city.

## 1.4 Major Anatomical Divisions of the Human Brain

| Division | Sub-structure | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Forebrain (Prosencephalon)** | Cerebrum | Higher cognition, memory, motor control |
| | Thalamus | Sensory relay station |
| | Hypothalamus | Homeostasis, hormone regulation |
| **Midbrain (Mesencephalon)** | Tectum, Tegmentum | Visual/audio reflexes, arousal |
| **Hindbrain (Rhombencephalon)** | Cerebellum | Motor coordination, balance |
| | Pons | Signal bridging |
| | Medulla Oblongata | Autonomic control (breathing, heart rate) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why study anatomy for ANN?** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"How does the human brain inspire artificial neural networks?"* The answer lies in three brain properties that ANNs attempt to mimic — **parallelism** (cerebrum), **adaptive learning** (synaptic plasticity), and **fault tolerance** (redundancy of neural pathways).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biological Neuron — Building Block of the Brain

A **biological neuron** consists of three functionally distinct regions:

1. **Dendrites** — Branch-like input receivers. They collect electrochemical signals from thousands of upstream neurons.
2. **Soma (Cell Body)** — The integration unit. It performs spatial and temporal summation of incoming postsynaptic potentials.
3. **Axon** — A long output fibre (length up to $\approx 1$ m in motor neurons) that conducts an **action potential** to downstream neurons via **synaptic terminals**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Signal Flow

- **Step 1 (Reception):** Neurotransmitters bind to receptors on the dendrites, opening ligand-gated ion channels.
- **Step 2 (Integration):** The soma integrates **Excitatory Postsynaptic Potentials (EPSPs)** and **Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potentials (IPSPs)**.
- **Step 3 (Thresholding):** If the net membrane potential $V_m$ exceeds the **threshold potential** ($\approx -55$ mV), an all-or-none **action potential** fires.
- **Step 4 (Propagation):** The action potential travels down the axon via saltatory conduction (jumping between Nodes of Ranvier in myelinated axons).
- **Step 5 (Transmission):** At the synaptic cleft, voltage-gated $Ca^{2+}$ channels trigger vesicle release of neurotransmitters (e.g., glutamate, GABA, dopamine).
- **Step 6 (Modulation):** The post-synaptic neuron's receptors respond, and the **synaptic weight** is updated based on correlated activity (basis of **Hebbian learning**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for ANNs:** Steps 1–3 map directly to a perceptron: **inputs ($x_i$)** → **weighted sum ($\sum w_i x_i + b$)** → **activation function (step/sigmoid/ReLU)**. Step 6 corresponds to **weight update rules** in gradient descent.

## 2.2 The Synapse — Where Learning Happens

A **synapse** is the junction between the **presynaptic** (axon terminal) and **postsynaptic** (dendrite/soma) neuron. Its efficacy — the **synaptic weight $w$** — is not fixed; it changes with experience through **Synaptic Plasticity**.

### 2.3 Membrane Potential — The Nernst & Goldman Equations

The resting membrane potential arises from ionic concentration gradients, primarily for $K^+$, $Na^+$, and $Cl^-$. Two key equations are examined in KTU-style derivations:

**Nernst Equation (single ion):**
$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation (multiple ions, resting state):**
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants used:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$ (universal gas constant), $F = 96{,}485 \, \text{C/mol}$ (Faraday's constant), $T$ in Kelvin, $z$ = ion valency.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and have appeared in similar forms in KTU Model Question Papers for neural-network subjects. Use them directly in derivations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Potential | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | $R$ = J/(mol·K), $F$ = C/mol, $z$ = ion charge | Deriving resting potential of a single ion |
| 2 | GHK Membrane Potential | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\dfrac{\sum P_i [i]_{out}}{\sum P_i [i]_{in}}\right)$ | $P_i$ = permeability (m/s) | Multi-ion resting potential |
| 3 | Conduction Velocity (Myelinated) | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | $f$ = firing freq. (Hz), $\lambda$ = internode length (m) | Speed of signal in saltatory conduction |
| 4 | Cable Equation (steady) | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m d}{4 r_i}}$ | $r_m, r_i$ = membrane/intrinsic resistivities | Length constant of a dendrite |
| 5 | Hebbian Weight Update | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j$ | $\eta$ = learning rate, $x$ = activity | Learning rule in ANNs |
| 6 | Synaptic Strength (STDP) | $\Delta w = A_+ e^{-\Delta t / \tau_+}$ if $\Delta t > 0$ | $A_+, \tau_+$ = constants | Spike-timing-dependent plasticity |
| 7 | Total Synapses in Brain | $N_{syn} = N_{neuron} \times \overline{c}$ | $\overline{c}$ ≈ $7{,}000$ avg. connections | Quantitative brain metrics |
| 8 | Brain Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{P_{total}}{M_{brain}}$ | W/kg | Energy efficiency comparison with computers |
| 9 | Threshold Function (Perceptron) | $y = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sum w_i x_i + b \geq \theta \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $b$ = bias, $\theta$ = threshold | Brain → ANN mapping |
| 10 | Sigmoid (smoothed threshold) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $z = \sum w_i x_i + b$ | Differentiable neuron model |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX safety rule for KTU notes:** In the markdown table above, absolute-value bars such as $\vert x \vert$ are written using `\vert` (and not the vertical pipe `\|`) to prevent table syntax breakage during PDF compilation.

## 2.5 Brain-Inspired Features Mapped to ANN

| Biological Feature | ANN Equivalent | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Synaptic weight $w_{ij}$ | Connection weight $w_{ij}$ | Stores learned knowledge |
| Firing rate (Hz) | Activation value $a$ | Output magnitude |
| Threshold $\theta$ | Bias $b$ | Decision boundary |
| Hebbian plasticity | Gradient descent / backprop | Learning algorithm |
| Parallel pathways | Distributed layers | Fault tolerance |
| Neurotransmitter types | Multiple activation functions | Non-linearity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive the equilibrium potential $E$ for a single ionic species across a permeable membrane.

**Step 1 — Start with the electrochemical potential.** The free energy change when one mole of ions of charge $z$ moves from inside ($in$) to outside ($out$) of a cell is the sum of the **chemical** and **electrical** contributions:

$$\Delta G = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) + z F (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

> Here, the first term is the chemical potential difference (concentration gradient), and the second is the electrical work done against the membrane potential.

**Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium condition.** At equilibrium, the net flux of ions is zero, meaning $\Delta G = 0$ (no further net movement):

$$0 = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) + z F (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the membrane potential.** By definition, $E = V_{in} - V_{out}$. Solving for $E$:

$$zF(V_{out} - V_{in}) = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

$$E = V_{in} - V_{out} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the Nernst Equation.** For $K^+$ at $37^\circ C$ with typical concentrations $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, the result evaluates to $E_{K} \approx -89$ mV, close to the resting potential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Evaluation of the Resting Potential for $K^+$

Given:
- $T = 310$ K (body temperature, $37^\circ C$)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $z = +1$ for $K^+$
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM

**Step 1 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\frac{RT}{zF} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the log ratio:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) = \ln(0.03571) = -3.332$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$E_{K} = 26.71 \times (-3.332) = -89.00 \text{ mV}$$

> The actual resting potential ($\approx -70$ mV) is less negative than $E_K$ because the membrane is also slightly permeable to $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$, which is handled by the **Goldman equation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The McCulloch–Pitts (MCP) Brain-Inspired Neuron

**Step 1 — Brain analogy.** A biological neuron either **fires** (action potential) or **does not fire**, based on whether the total input exceeds a threshold.

**Step 2 — Formal mathematical model (1943):** A binary neuron with $n$ inputs:

$$y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - \theta\right)$$

where the **activation function** is the Heaviside step:

$$f(net) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } net \geq 0 \\ 0, & \text{if } net < 0 \end{cases}$$

> Here, $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ are binary inputs, $w_i$ are fixed or learned synaptic weights, and $\theta$ is the firing threshold.

**Step 3 — Logical computation capability.** The MCP neuron can realize any linearly separable Boolean function. For example, the **AND gate** is obtained with $w_1 = w_2 = 1$ and $\theta = 1.5$:

- $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow net = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5 \geq 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$
- $x_1 = 0, x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow net = 1 - 1.5 = -0.5 < 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$ (and symmetrically for other cases)

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Module 1:** This is the precise mathematical bridge from the **biological neuron** (human brain) to the **artificial neuron** that students will extend in later modules with backpropagation and multi-layer architectures.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hebbian Learning Rule from Biological Observation

**Biological postulate (Donald Hebb, 1949):**
> *"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells…."*

**Step 1 — Mathematical translation.** The change in synaptic weight $\Delta w_{ij}$ between presynaptic neuron $i$ and postsynaptic neuron $j$ is proportional to the product of their activations:

$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j$$

**Step 2 — Verification for correlated activity.** If both $x_i$ and $x_j$ are high (correlated), $w_{ij}$ grows — the connection is **strengthened**. If one is silent, $\Delta w = 0$ — the connection does not change. This reproduces long-term potentiation (LTP) observed in the hippocampus.

**Step 3 — Normalised form used in ANNs:** To prevent unbounded weight growth:

$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j - \eta \cdot w_{ij} \cdot x_i^2$$

> The second term is a **decay/regularisation** term that emulates biological forgetting.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Brain vs. ANN

| Feature | Human Brain | Artificial Neural Network |
|---|---|---|
| Basic unit | Biological neuron (with dendrites, soma, axon) | Artificial neuron (MCP / Perceptron) |
| Number of units | $\approx 86 \times 10^9$ | $10^2$ – $10^8$ (typical deep network) |
| Connection mechanism | Synapse (electrochemical) | Weighted edge (numerical) |
| Signal type | Action potential (frequency-modulated) | Floating-point number |
| Speed | $\approx 100$ Hz per neuron | $10^9$ Hz (GPU clock) |
| Power | $\approx 20$ W | $\approx 300$ W (training a large model) |
| Learning | Synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD, STDP) | Backpropagation, gradient descent |
| Fault tolerance | Very high (localised damage compensable) | Low (precision-sensitive) |
| Generalisation | Excellent, one-/few-shot | Requires large datasets |
| Knowledge storage | Distributed across synapses | Distributed across weights |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Human Brain (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Sensory Input: Visual Cortex"]
    inputB["Sensory Input: Auditory Cortex"]
    inputC["Sensory Input: Somatosensory Cortex"]
    thalamus["Thalamus: Sensory Relay and Filter"]
    cerebrumA["Frontal Lobe: Decision and Planning"]
    cerebrumB["Parietal Lobe: Spatial Reasoning"]
    cerebrumC["Temporal Lobe: Memory and Language"]
    cerebrumD["Occipital Lobe: Visual Processing"]
    cerebellum["Cerebellum: Motor Coordination and Timing"]
    hippocampus["Hippocampus: Memory Formation"]
    amygdala["Amygdala: Emotion and Fear Response"]
    brainstem["Brainstem: Autonomic Control"]
    motor["Motor Cortex: Output to Muscles"]
    feedback["Feedback Loops: Continuous Learning"]

    inputA --> thalamus
    inputB --> thalamus
    inputC --> thalamus
    thalamus --> cerebrumA
    thalamus --> cerebrumB
    thalamus --> cerebrumC
    thalamus --> cerebrumD
    cerebrumA --> hippocampus
    cerebrumC --> hippocampus
    cerebrumA --> amygdala
    cerebrumC --> amygdala
    cerebrumA --> motor
    cerebrumD --> motor
    motor --> cerebellum
    cerebellum --> feedback
    feedback --> cerebrumA
    cerebrumA --> brainstem
    amygdala --> brainstem
```

> The diagram above abstracts the major cortical and sub-cortical regions of the human brain as a **functional pipeline**, showing how sensory inputs are relayed, integrated, memorised, and transformed into motor outputs — exactly the kind of schematic expected in KTU Module 1 descriptive answers.

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Biological Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dend1["Dendrite 1: Input Channel"]
    dend2["Dendrite 2: Input Channel"]
    dend3["Dendrite N: Input Channel"]
    soma["Soma: Integration and Thresholding"]
    axonH["Axon Hillock: Action Potential Initiation"]
    axon["Axon: Signal Conduction"]
    myelin["Myelin Sheath and Nodes of Ranvier"]
    terminal["Synaptic Terminal: Neurotransmitter Release"]
    cleft["Synaptic Cleft: Chemical Gap"]
    postSyn["Postsynaptic Neuron: Next Cell in Chain"]

    dend1 --> soma
    dend2 --> soma
    dend3 --> soma
    soma --> axonH
    axonH --> axon
    axon --> myelin
    myelin --> terminal
    terminal --> cleft
    cleft --> postSyn
```

> This functional block diagram maps the *signal-flow architecture* of a biological neuron — the conceptual ancestor of the artificial neuron used throughout the rest of the course.

## 4.3 Mapping from Biological Neuron to Artificial Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bioInput["Biological: Dendritic Inputs x1 to xN"]
    bioWeight["Biological: Synaptic Strength w1 to wN"]
    bioSum["Biological: Spatial and Temporal Summation in Soma"]
    bioThresh["Biological: Threshold Check at Axon Hillock"]
    bioOutput["Biological: Action Potential Frequency"]

    annInput["ANN: Input Vector x"]
    annWeight["ANN: Weight Matrix W"]
    annSum["ANN: Weighted Sum plus Bias"]
    annAct["ANN: Activation Function sigma"]
    annOutput["ANN: Scalar or Vector Activation"]

    bioInput --> bioWeight
    bioWeight --> bioSum
    bioSum --> bioThresh
    bioThresh --> bioOutput

    annInput --> annWeight
    annWeight --> annSum
    annSum --> annAct
    annAct --> annOutput

    bioInput -.mapped to.-> annInput
    bioWeight -.mapped to.-> annWeight
    bioSum -.mapped to.-> annSum
    bioThresh -.mapped to.-> annAct
    bioOutput -.mapped to.-> annOutput
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This conceptual mapping is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU Module 1. Memorise the correspondence: dendrite $\leftrightarrow$ input, synapse $\leftrightarrow$ weight, soma $\leftrightarrow$ summation, axon hillock $\leftrightarrow$ activation function, action-potential rate $\leftrightarrow$ output activation.

## 4.4 Neural Signal Propagation — Saltatory Conduction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start["Resting State: Vm = minus 70 mV"]
    stim["Stimulus Arrives at Dendrite"]
    depol["Depolarisation: Vm rises toward minus 55 mV"]
    threshold["Threshold Crossed at Axon Hillock"]
    ap["Action Potential Fires: Vm peaks at plus 30 mV"]
    propa["Propagation along Axon via Saltatory Conduction"]
    term["Arrival at Synaptic Terminal"]
    ca["Voltage Gated Ca2 plus Channels Open"]
    release["Neurotransmitter Vesicles Released"]
    bind["Neurotransmitters Bind to Postsynaptic Receptors"]
    psp["EPSP or IPSP Generated on Next Neuron"]

    start --> stim
    stim --> depol
    depol --> threshold
    threshold --> ap
    ap --> propa
    propa --> term
    term --> ca
    ca --> release
    release --> bind
    bind --> psp
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`
**Explain in brief the structure of a biological neuron. How does it act as the inspiration for an artificial neuron? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

A biological neuron consists of three parts:
1. **Dendrites** — receive input signals from other neurons in the form of electrochemical impulses.
2. **Soma (cell body)** — integrates the incoming signals; if the integrated potential exceeds a threshold, the neuron fires.
3. **Axon** — conducts the resulting action potential to the synaptic terminals, which release neurotransmitters onto the next neuron's dendrites.

It inspires the **artificial neuron** as follows: dendrites correspond to inputs $x_i$, the synaptic strengths to weights $w_i$, the soma to the summation unit $\sum w_i x_i + b$, and the firing threshold to the activation function. Hence, the biological neuron is the direct biological analogue of the McCulloch–Pitts/Perceptron model.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]`
**State and explain the Nernst equation. What is its significance in determining the resting potential of a neuron? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential of a single ion across a semipermeable membrane:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

It is derived by equating the chemical free-energy gradient (due to concentration difference) with the electrical work required to move the ion across the membrane. **Significance:** it provides the theoretical membrane potential that would result if the membrane were permeable to *only* one ion. For example, the Nernst potential for $K^+$ at body temperature is about $-89$ mV, very close to the actual resting potential ($\approx -70$ mV), confirming that the resting neuron is predominantly $K^+$-permeable.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Equivalent, Module 1]`
**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the structure of a biological neuron and explain each of its parts. Discuss how the biological neuron acts as the inspiration for an artificial neuron. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of an ionic species across a semi-permeable membrane. Using it, compute the equilibrium potential for $K^+$ at $37^\circ C$, given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Biological Neuron Structure (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** for the labelled functional diagram. The verbal description should cover:

- **Dendrites** (1 mark) — tree-like projections that act as input channels; they receive neurotransmitters from upstream neurons and convert them into graded local potentials.
- **Soma** (2 marks) — the cell body that houses the nucleus and performs **spatial summation** (summing inputs from different dendrites) and **temporal summation** (summing inputs arriving in quick succession). If the integrated membrane potential crosses the **threshold** ($\approx -55$ mV), an all-or-none **action potential** is triggered at the **axon hillock**.
- **Axon and Synaptic Terminals** (2 marks) — the axon conducts the action potential via saltatory conduction in myelinated fibres; at the terminals, voltage-gated $Ca^{2+}$ channels open, triggering the release of neurotransmitter vesicles into the synaptic cleft.
- **Inspiration for ANN** (2 marks) — each biological component maps to a mathematical element: dendrite $\to$ input $x_i$, synapse $\to$ weight $w_i$, soma $\to$ weighted summation $\sum w_i x_i + b$, threshold $\to$ activation function $f(\cdot)$, and action-potential frequency $\to$ output activation. This is the foundation of the **Perceptron** model.

**(b) Nernst Equation Derivation and Computation (7 Marks):**

The detailed derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The marking allocation is:

- Statement of electrochemical free energy: (2 marks)
- Setting $\Delta G = 0$ at equilibrium: (1 mark)
- Final algebraic manipulation to obtain $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$: (2 marks)
- Numerical substitution: (1 mark) → prefactor $\frac{RT}{F} = 26.71$ mV.
- Final answer: (1 mark) → $E_{K} = 26.71 \times \ln(5/140) = -89.0$ mV.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Equivalent, Module 1]`
**(a)** Explain the major anatomical divisions of the human brain and discuss how each contributes to specific computational or cognitive functions relevant to artificial neural network design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to Hebb's postulate, derive the Hebbian learning rule. Show how this rule can be implemented as a weight-update equation in an artificial neural network, and discuss its biological validity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Major Brain Divisions and Their Computational Roles (7 Marks):**

| Division | Sub-part | Function | ANN Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forebrain | Cerebrum (frontal lobe) | Decision making, planning, motor control | Output/policy layer in deep networks |
| Forebrain | Cerebrum (parietal lobe) | Spatial integration of sensory data | Feature integration layer |
| Forebrain | Cerebrum (temporal lobe) | Memory storage and language | Long-term memory weights (LSTM analogy) |
| Forebrain | Thalamus | Sensory relay and gating | Input pre-processing layer |
| Midbrain | Tectum/Tegmentum | Reflexive attention | Attention mechanism in transformers |
| Hindbrain | Cerebellum | Motor timing and error correction | Feedback/recurrent connections |
| Hindbrain | Medulla | Autonomic control | Hard-wired control loops |

> **Marker-friendly tip:** Use a table like the above to clearly establish the *brain → ANN* analogy. KTU examiners award 1 mark per valid mapping (up to 7).

**(b) Hebbian Learning Rule Derivation (7 Marks):**

- **Biological postulate** (1 mark): Hebb's rule — *"neurons that fire together, wire together."*
- **Mathematical translation** (2 marks): Let $x_i$ and $x_j$ be the firing rates of pre- and post-synaptic neurons. The change in synaptic strength is proportional to their correlated activity:
$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j$$
- **Normalisation / decay term** (1 mark): To prevent unbounded growth, biological synapses exhibit depression:
$$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j - \gamma \cdot w_{ij}$$
- **ANN implementation** (2 marks): The Hebbian rule is the precursor of unsupervised learning rules such as **Oja's rule** and **Sanger's rule**, and it appears in modern self-organising maps and STDP-based spiking neural networks. In standard backpropagation, the weight update $\Delta w = -\eta \partial E / \partial w$ differs in that it uses a *target signal*, whereas Hebbian learning is purely *local* and *correlation-based*.
- **Biological validity** (1 mark): Long-Term Potentiation (LTP) experiments in the hippocampus by Bliss & Lømo (1973) confirmed that high-frequency correlated pre- and post-synaptic activity indeed strengthens synapses, validating Hebb's postulate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Human Brain" Questions**
> 1. **Confusing $E_K$ with the resting $V_m$** — students often quote $-70$ mV as the Nernst potential; it is actually the resting potential *after* multi-ion permeability. The Nernst potential for $K^+$ alone is $-89$ mV.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in Nernst equation computations — always include mV or V. Examiners deduct 1 mark if units are missing.
> 3. **Vague brain-to-ANN mappings** — statements like *"the brain is like a computer"* earn zero marks. Use the explicit correspondence: *dendrite → input, synapse → weight, soma → summation, threshold → activation*.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark answers** — a labelled functional block diagram is mandatory; without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Treating Hebbian rule as identical to gradient descent** — they are fundamentally different: Hebbian is local and unsupervised, while backpropagation is global and supervised.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Human Brain" (Module 1)**

- [x] **Brain mass:** $\approx 1.35$ kg, $\approx 2\%$ of body weight; consumes $\approx 20$ W.
- [x] **Neurons:** $\approx 86 \times 10^9$; glial cells $\approx 85 \times 10^9$.
- [x] **Synapses per neuron (avg.):** $\approx 7{,}000$ → total $\approx 10^{14}$–$10^{15}$.
- [x] **Resting potential:** $\approx -70$ mV; threshold $\approx -55$ mV; action-potential peak $\approx +30$ mV.
- [x] **Three parts of a biological neuron:** **Dendrites** (input), **Soma** (integration), **Axon** (output).
- [x] **Action potential mechanism:** Depolarisation → $Na^+$ influx → repolarisation → $K^+$ efflux → hyperpolarisation → refractory period.
- [x] **Nernst equation:** $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$; numerical value at $310$ K: $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71$ mV.
- [x] **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz equation:** accounts for multi-ion permeability; explains $-70$ mV resting potential.
- [x] **Saltatory conduction:** in myelinated axons, signal jumps between Nodes of Ranvier; speed up to $\approx 120$ m/s.
- [x] **Hebb's postulate:** "Neurons that fire together, wire together" — $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot x_i \cdot x_j$.
- [x] **McCulloch–Pitts (1943):** first mathematical model of an artificial neuron; binary threshold logic.
- [x] **Brain → ANN mapping (must memorise):**
   - Dendrite → Input $x_i$
   - Synapse → Weight $w_i$
   - Soma → Summation $\sum w_i x_i + b$
   - Axon hillock → Activation function $f(\cdot)$
   - Action-potential rate → Output activation
- [x] **Brain advantages exploited by ANNs:** Parallelism, distributed representation, fault tolerance, learning from experience.
- [x] **Brain limitations computers don't share:** Slow ($\approx 100$ Hz), imprecise, but **energy-efficient** and **sample-efficient** (one-shot/few-shot learning).
- [x] **Key historical names:** **Donald Hebb** (1949), **McCulloch & Pitts** (1943), **Rosenblatt** (Perceptron, 1958).
- [x] **Energy efficiency comparison:** Brain $\approx 20$ W; equivalent ANN simulation requires $10^4$–$10^6$ times more power — a motivation for neuromorphic computing.
- [x] **Common exam keywords to use:** *graded potential, all-or-none principle, refractory period, saltatory conduction, synaptic plasticity, LTP/LTD, Hebbian learning, MCP neuron.*

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam strategy for Module 1 — "Human Brain":**
> 1. Always draw a **labelled diagram** of the biological neuron (worth up to 2 marks by itself).
> 2. For derivation questions, write the **starting equation**, state the **assumption** (e.g., $\Delta G = 0$ at equilibrium), and show **every algebraic step** — KTU examiners allocate partial marks generously.
> 3. Memorise the numerical values in **bold** in the cheat sheet above; at least one numerical question appears in every KTU ESE paper.
> 4. Always close your answer with the **brain-to-ANN mapping table** to demonstrate conceptual maturity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model of an Artificial Neuron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT522 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Model of an Artificial Neuron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> An **Artificial Neuron** (also called a *neurode*, *node*, or *unit*) is a fundamental computational element of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) that mimics the information-processing behavior of a biological neuron. It receives one or more inputs, multiplies each input by a corresponding **synaptic weight**, sums the weighted inputs, adds a **bias term**, and passes the result through a nonlinear **activation function** to produce a single scalar output.

Mathematically, a single artificial neuron implements the following functional mapping:

$$
y = \varphi \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b \right)
$$

where the symbols have exact KTU-significance meanings detailed in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECAT522 Module 1):**
> Students must be able to **(i)** draw and label the block diagram of an artificial neuron, **(ii)** identify the role of weights, bias, summation unit, and activation function, and **(iii)** distinguish between McCulloch-Pitts, Perceptron, and the general neuron models.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **classroom teacher** deciding whether to declare a holiday. The teacher gathers several inputs:

- **Input 1 (x₁):** Weather forecast (Heavy rain? Sunny?)
- **Input 2 (x₂):** Local holiday calendar (Already a public holiday?)
- **Input 3 (x₃):** School event scheduled? (Yes/No)

Each factor is *not equally important*. The teacher subconsciously **assigns importance (weights)** to each signal:

- Heavy rain → **High importance** (w₁ = 0.7)
- Calendar clash → **Medium importance** (w₂ = 0.4)
- School event → **Lower importance** (w₃ = 0.2)

The teacher then **adds up all the weighted signals**, applies a **personal threshold (bias)**, and finally **takes a decision** (Holiday / No Holiday) — this decision is the **activation function**.

This *real-world* decision-making process is **precisely** what an artificial neuron does — it is a **weighted-decision-engine** that converts a vector of inputs into a single meaningful output.

> [!TIP]
> **The Biological Inspiration:** A real human neuron has *dendrites* (input lines), a *cell body* (summation unit), and an *axon* (output). The *synapse* is the connection strength — analogous to the **weight** $w_i$. The *firing threshold* is analogous to the **bias** $b$.

### 1.3 Why Artificial Neurons Matter in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Use of Artificial Neuron |
|---|---|
| **Pattern Recognition** | Single neuron = linear classifier |
| **Control Systems** | Single neuron as an adaptive threshold detector |
| **Signal Processing** | Neuron as a tunable filter (e.g., LMS adaptive filter) |
| **Embedded AI** | Single neuron = small footprint inference engine |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of an Artificial Neuron

An artificial neuron is built from **five functional stages**. Each stage is mandatory for a complete KTU board-answer.

**Stage 1 — Input Layer (Receiving the Signal):**
- The neuron receives $n$ input signals, denoted as the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$.
- These may be raw sensor values, pixel intensities, or pre-processed features.

**Stage 2 — Synaptic Weighting (Connection Strength):**
- Each input $x_i$ is multiplied by a **synaptic weight** $w_i$.
- The set of weights is the **learnable parameter** of the neuron — these are what training algorithms adjust.
- Mathematically: weighted input = $w_i \cdot x_i$.

**Stage 3 — Summation Unit (Linear Combiner / Adder):**
- All weighted inputs are summed: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$.
- This forms the **net input** before the bias and activation are applied.

**Stage 4 — Bias Addition (Threshold Shift):**
- A scalar $b$ is added to shift the activation function left or right along the input axis.
- **Why?** Without a bias, the decision boundary of a single neuron would be forced to pass through the origin — which is rarely optimal for real data.
- The complete pre-activation quantity is called the **local induced field** or **net input** $v$:

$$
v = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b
$$

**Stage 5 — Activation Function (Nonlinearity):**
- The net input $v$ is passed through $\varphi(\cdot)$ to produce the final output $y$.
- **Why a nonlinearity?** Without $\varphi$, stacking neurons would collapse mathematically into a single linear equation — the network could not learn complex patterns. This is the **Universal Approximation** reason.

### 2.2 Common Activation Functions (KTU Exam Favourites)

| Activation Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Use Case (KTU context) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Step (Heaviside)** | $\varphi(v) = \begin{cases} 1 & v \geq 0 \\ 0 & v < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | — | McCulloch-Pitts, Perceptron |
| **Sign (Bipolar Step)** | $\varphi(v) = \text{sgn}(v)$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ | — | Perceptron (bipolar) |
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\varphi(v) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-v}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\varphi(v)(1-\varphi(v))$ | Backprop networks |
| **Hyperbolic Tangent** | $\varphi(v) = \tanh(v)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1-\tanh^2(v)$ | Zero-centered output |
| **ReLU** | $\varphi(v) = \max(0, v)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\begin{cases} 0 & v<0 \\ 1 & v \geq 0 \end{cases}$ | Modern deep networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** For 3-mark and 7-mark questions, *always* mention the **range** of the activation function and whether it is **linear or nonlinear** — these are recurring valuation hot-spots.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Standard Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_i$ | $i$-th Input | Numerical input value | Dimensionless (normalized) |
| $w_i$ | $i$-th Weight | Synaptic strength of $i$-th connection | Real scalar |
| $\mathbf{w}$ | Weight Vector | $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, \dots, w_n]^T$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $\mathbf{x}$ | Input Vector | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, \dots, x_n]^T$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $b$ | Bias | Threshold shifter / offset | Real scalar |
| $v$ | Net Input (Induced Field) | $v = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$ | Real scalar |
| $\varphi(\cdot)$ | Activation Function | Nonlinearity applied to $v$ | Function |
| $y$ | Output | $y = \varphi(v)$ | Real scalar |
| $n$ | Input Dimension | Number of inputs to neuron | Positive integer |
| $\theta$ | Threshold (old notation) | Equivalent to $-b$ | Real scalar |
| $\eta$ | Learning Rate | Step size in weight update | $\in (0, 1]$ |
| $t$ | Target (Desired Output) | Ground truth label | Real scalar |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **A single neuron is a linear binary classifier.** It draws a single **hyperplane** $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ that separates the input space into two halves.
- This hyperplane's orientation is governed by $\mathbf{w}$ and its offset by $b$.
- **Stacked neurons** (a network) can carve *arbitrarily complex* decision regions — the foundation of deep learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Biological Neuron to Mathematical Model

We start with the structure of a **biological neuron** and systematically abstract each component to a mathematical primitive.

**Step 1 — Identify biological components:**

| Biological Part | Function | Mathematical Abstraction |
|---|---|---|
| Dendrites | Receive signals | Inputs $x_1, \dots, x_n$ |
| Synapse | Modulate signal strength | Weights $w_1, \dots, w_n$ |
| Cell body (Soma) | Aggregate signals | Summation $\sum$ |
| Axon hillock | Decide to fire | Activation function $\varphi$ |
| Axon | Transmit output | Output $y$ |

**Step 2 — Model the synaptic modulation:**

Each dendrite $i$ carries a signal $x_i$ that is scaled by the synaptic efficacy $w_i$. The *received contribution* at the soma is:

$$
\text{contribution}_i = w_i \cdot x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate at the soma (linear combination):**

All received contributions are summed:

$$
u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i
$$

Writing this compactly in vector form:

$$
u = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 & w_2 & \dots & w_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i
$$

**Step 4 — Add the bias (mimicking the firing threshold):**

A bias $b$ is introduced to shift the activation threshold. The **net input** (also called *induced local field* or *activation potential*) becomes:

$$
v = u + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b
$$

Equivalently:

$$
v = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the activation function:**

The neuron "fires" only if $v$ exceeds an internal threshold. We capture this with a nonlinear function $\varphi$:

$$
y = \varphi(v) = \varphi\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b \right)
$$

This is the **canonical mathematical model of an artificial neuron** that KTU expects in every answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold vs. Bias Equivalence:** In older texts (McCulloch-Pitts, 1943; Rosenblatt, 1958), a *threshold* $\theta$ is subtracted from the sum: $y = \varphi\left( \sum w_i x_i - \theta \right)$. Setting $b = -\theta$ makes the two notations **mathematically identical**. KTU accepts both, but the *bias form* $y = \varphi(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$ is the modern standard.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Single-Neuron Computation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Consider an artificial neuron with $n=2$ inputs, weights $w_1 = 0.6$, $w_2 = -0.4$, bias $b = 0.2$, and a **sigmoid activation function**. Given inputs $x_1 = 1.0$, $x_2 = 0.5$, compute the output $y$.

**Solution — Step-by-step:**

**Step A — Compute the linear combination:**

$$
u = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2
$$

Substituting values:

$$
u = (0.6)(1.0) + (-0.4)(0.5) = 0.6 - 0.2 = 0.4
$$

**Step B — Add the bias to form the net input:**

$$
v = u + b = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6
$$

**Step C — Apply the sigmoid activation function:**

$$
y = \varphi(v) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.6}}
$$

Evaluating the exponential:

$$
e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488
$$

Substituting back:

$$
y = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5488} = \frac{1}{1.5488} \approx 0.6457
$$

**Step D — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{y \approx 0.6457}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *"compute the output of an artificial neuron"*, always present the four sub-steps (linear sum → net input → activation → output) as a **numbered list**. Examiners award 1 mark per logical step.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Classical Neuron Models

| Feature | McCulloch-Pitts (1943) | Perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958) | General / Modern Neuron |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inputs** | Binary $\{0,1\}$ | Real-valued | Real-valued |
| **Weights** | Fixed (pre-assigned) | **Learned** via delta rule | **Learned** via gradient descent |
| **Bias / Threshold** | Threshold $\theta$ | Threshold $\theta$ | Bias $b$ |
| **Activation** | Step (binary output) | Step (bipolar or unipolar) | Any (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh, …) |
| **Learnable?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Output** | $\{0, 1\}$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ or $\{0, 1\}$ | Continuous |
| **Limitation** | Cannot learn weights | Cannot solve XOR | Solves XOR (with hidden layers) |
| **KTU Relevance** | Historical foundation | 14-mark favorite | Most asked form |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of an Artificial Neuron

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *complete signal flow* of a single artificial neuron, including all five functional stages. This is the standard diagram examiners expect in any "draw the model of an artificial neuron" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["x1"] --> MUL1
    W1["w1"] --> MUL1
    MUL1 --> SUM["Summation Unit v = sum wi xi + b"]
    X2["x2"] --> MUL2
    W2["w2"] --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> SUM
    X3["x3"] --> MUL3
    W3["w3"] --> MUL3
    MUL3 --> SUM
    B["Bias b"] --> SUM
    SUM --> ACT["Activation Function phi"]
    ACT --> OUT["Output y = phi v"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below shows a *deeper* functional view, breaking out the summation, bias injection, and decision stages separately — useful for 14-mark "explain with block diagram" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT STAGE"]
        X1["Input x1"] 
        X2["Input x2"] 
        X3["Input xn"]
    end
    
    subgraph WT["WEIGHTING STAGE"]
        X1 --> M1["Multiply by w1"]
        X2 --> M2["Multiply by w2"]
        X3 --> M3["Multiply by wn"]
    end
    
    M1 --> S["Linear Combiner u = sum wi xi"]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    B["Bias b"] --> S
    
    S --> N["Net Input v = u + b"]
    N --> P["Activation phi"]
    P --> Y["Final Output y"]
```

### 4.3 Annotated Component Roles

| Block | Role | Symbol | Adjustable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Stage** | Carries external data into the neuron | $x_i$ | No (given) |
| **Weighting Stage** | Modulates input importance | $w_i$ | ✅ Yes (learned) |
| **Linear Combiner** | Sums weighted inputs | $u = \sum w_i x_i$ | Depends on $w_i$ |
| **Bias** | Shifts decision threshold | $b$ | ✅ Yes (learned) |
| **Net Input** | Pre-activation scalar | $v$ | — |
| **Activation** | Introduces nonlinearity | $\varphi(\cdot)$ | Function choice |
| **Output** | Final neuron response | $y$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

> *Define the mathematical model of an artificial neuron and explain the role of the activation function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An artificial neuron is a mathematical model of a biological neuron that computes:

$$
y = \varphi \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b \right)
$$

where $x_i$ are the inputs, $w_i$ are the **synaptic weights**, $b$ is the **bias**, $\varphi(\cdot)$ is the **activation function**, and $y$ is the final output. **(2 marks)**

The **activation function** introduces **nonlinearity** into the neuron's response, enabling the network to learn and represent complex, non-linear input-output mappings. Without it, the neuron would behave as a pure linear combiner and the network could not solve non-linearly separable problems such as the XOR function. Common activations include the *step*, *sigmoid*, *tanh*, and *ReLU* functions. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

> *List the five functional stages of an artificial neuron. What is the significance of the bias term?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five functional stages of an artificial neuron are:
1. **Input stage** – receives the input signals $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
2. **Synaptic weighting stage** – multiplies each input by its corresponding weight $w_i$.
3. **Summation stage** – computes the linear combination $\sum w_i x_i$.
4. **Bias addition stage** – adds the bias $b$ to the sum.
5. **Activation stage** – applies the nonlinear activation $\varphi(\cdot)$ to produce output $y$. **(2 marks)**

The **bias term $b$** acts as a *threshold shifter*. It allows the decision boundary of the neuron to be translated away from the origin of the input space. Without a bias, a single-neuron classifier would be forced to pass its hyperplane through the origin, which severely limits its representational power. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module 1 – Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Style]**

> **(a)** Draw the block diagram of an artificial neuron and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a neuron with two inputs $x_1 = 0.8$ and $x_2 = -0.5$, weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.9$, bias $b = 0.1$, and a **sigmoid activation** $\varphi(v) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-v}}$. Compute the final output $y$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The block diagram consists of the following blocks connected in series (see Section 4.1 mermaid for the visual). For marks, students must label every block clearly.

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Input nodes $x_1, \dots, x_n$ | Carry input signals to the neuron | 1 |
| Synaptic weights $w_1, \dots, w_n$ | Scale each input; learned during training | 1 |
| Summation unit | Computes $\sum w_i x_i$ | 1 |
| Bias $b$ | Adds offset to shift threshold | 1 |
| Activation function $\varphi$ | Introduces nonlinearity, decides output | 1.5 |
| Output $y$ | Final neuron response | 0.5 |
| Diagram quality (axes, arrows, neatness) | — | 1 |

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute the linear combination.

$$
u = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 = (0.5)(0.8) + (0.9)(-0.5) = 0.4 - 0.45 = -0.05
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2:** Add the bias to form the net input.

$$
v = u + b = -0.05 + 0.1 = 0.05
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Apply the sigmoid activation.

$$
y = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.05}}
$$

Evaluate the exponential:

$$
e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512
$$

Substitute:

$$
y = \frac{1}{1 + 0.9512} = \frac{1}{1.9512} \approx 0.5125
$$

*(3.5 marks)*

**Step 4:** State the final answer.

$$
\boxed{y \approx 0.5125}
$$

*(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Style]**

> **(a)** Compare the McCulloch-Pitts, Perceptron, and the general (modern) artificial neuron models in terms of inputs, weights, activation function, and learning capability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For an artificial neuron with three inputs $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0, 1]^T$, weights $\mathbf{w} = [0.3, -0.7, 0.5]^T$, bias $b = -0.2$, and a **signum activation** $\varphi(v) = \text{sgn}(v)$, compute the output $y$. State the sign of the output and the binary class it represents. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

A clean *comparison table* is expected. For each row, students must justify the entry (marks awarded per cell).

| Property | McCulloch-Pitts | Perceptron | General Neuron |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inputs** | Binary $\{0,1\}$ (1.25 marks) | Real-valued (0.5 mark) | Real-valued (0.5 mark) |
| **Weights** | Fixed, hand-designed (1 mark) | Learned via Perceptron rule (1 mark) | Learned via gradient descent (0.5 mark) |
| **Activation** | Step / threshold (0.75 mark) | Step (bipolar or unipolar) (0.5 mark) | Sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, etc. (0.5 mark) |
| **Learning** | No learning — hard-wired (0.5 mark) | Online, single-layer (0.5 mark) | Supervised, multi-layer capable (0.5 mark) |

**Total: 7 marks**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute the dot product $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$.

$$
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} = (0.3)(1) + (-0.7)(0) + (0.5)(1) = 0.3 + 0 + 0.5 = 0.8
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2:** Add the bias.

$$
v = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0.8 + (-0.2) = 0.6
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3:** Apply the signum activation.

$$
\varphi(v) = \text{sgn}(0.6) = +1
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4:** Interpret the output.

$$
\boxed{y = +1}
$$

The output is **positive** and corresponds to the **positive class** (Class +1). The neuron classifies the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0, 1]^T$ as belonging to the **+1 category** of the bipolar scheme. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** — a frequent 1-mark loss. Even if $b = 0$ is not stated, the formula **must** include it for full marks.
> 2. **Confusing *threshold* $\theta$ with *bias* $b$** — remember $\theta = -b$. Examiners may deduct 0.5 marks for not stating the equivalence.
> 3. **Skipping the activation function step in numerical problems** — if the question gives a specific $\varphi(\cdot)$, you *must* apply it and *show* the evaluation. A bare "compute the sum" loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Sign error in $e^{-v}$** — when $v$ is negative, $e^{-v} > 1$, not $< 1$. Double-check signs in sigmoid and tanh evaluations.
> 5. **Not drawing arrows in the block diagram** — examiners look for **directional flow** from input to output. Static boxes without arrows lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Calling the activation function "linear"** — ReLU is *piecewise linear*, not linear in the strict sense. Sigmoid and tanh are *nonlinear*. Be precise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **mathematical model of an artificial neuron** is $y = \varphi(\sum w_i x_i + b)$.
- ✅ The **five stages** are: *Input → Weighting → Summation → Bias addition → Activation*.
- ✅ **Weights** are *learnable*; inputs and activation function choice are *pre-defined*.
- ✅ **Bias** shifts the decision boundary away from the origin — without it, only origin-passing hyperplanes are possible.
- ✅ **Activation function** injects **nonlinearity** — essential for representing complex functions.
- ✅ The **McCulloch-Pitts model** uses *fixed* binary weights and step activation; the **Perceptron** adds *learnable* weights; the **general neuron** uses *real-valued* inputs and *differentiable* activations.
- ✅ A single artificial neuron is a **linear binary classifier** that draws a *hyperplane* $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$.
- ✅ **Threshold** ($\theta$) and **bias** ($b$) are equivalent up to a sign: $\theta = -b$.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark numericals, the marking scheme is roughly: *formula (1 m) + weighted sum (2 m) + bias addition (1 m) + activation application (2 m) + final answer (1 m)*.
- ✅ **Always** show units or state "dimensionless" if inputs are normalized — examiners notice this.
- ✅ **Activation function range** is a high-yield fact: sigmoid $\rightarrow (0,1)$, tanh $\rightarrow (-1,1)$, ReLU $\rightarrow [0,\infty)$, step $\rightarrow \{0,1\}$, signum $\rightarrow \{-1,+1\}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT522 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Neural Networks ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Artificial Neural Network (ANN)? An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a massively parallel distributed information processing system modeled after the biological nervous system (the human brain). It consists of simple processing units called **artificial neurons** or **nodes** that are interconnected via **weighted links** (synapses) and is designed to learn from observed data through iterative adjustment of these connection weights. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus definition, an ANN is a computational paradigm inspired by the structure and functional aspects of biological neural networks, which uses connected units (neurons) to perform pattern recognition, function approximation, classification, clustering, and prediction tasks without requiring explicit programmed rules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** The primary Course Outcome for Module 1 is the ability to *"describe the fundamental concepts, biological inspiration, and architectural models of artificial neural networks."* This module is foundational and forms the basis for perceptron learning, backpropagation, and deep networks covered in later modules. ### 1.2 The Biological Neuron — The Inspiration The human brain contains approximately **86 billion neurons**, each forming on average **10,000 synaptic connections** with other neurons. A biological neuron consists of three principal parts: | Biological Component | Functional Role | |---|---| | **Dendrites** | Tree-like receptive structures that collect input signals from other neurons | | **Soma (Cell Body)** | Sums the incoming electrical signals and decides whether to fire | | **Axon** | The transmission line that carries the output electrical impulse to other neurons | | **Synapse** | The junction point that modulates the strength of the signal passed to the next neuron | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a biological neuron like a *village panchayat president*. They listen to multiple suggestions (dendrites), weigh their importance, and based on the cumulative pressure, decide whether to act (fire or stay silent). Artificial neurons mimic this exact behavior mathematically. ### 1.3 The Artificial Neuron (McCulloch-Pitts Model) The **McCulloch-Pitts (MCP) Neuron** (1943) is the earliest mathematical model of an artificial neuron. It is a **binary threshold unit** that: - Receives multiple binary inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \in \{0, 1\}$ - Computes a weighted sum: $\text{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i$ - Compares this sum against a **threshold** $\theta$ - Outputs $y = 1$ if $\text{net} \geq \theta$, otherwise $y = 0$ This can be compactly written as: $$y = f(\text{net}) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \geq \theta \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** The MCP model uses **fixed, non-adaptive weights** — it cannot *learn*. Learning was introduced later by **Rosenblatt's Perceptron (1958)**, where the weights are adjusted based on the error between predicted and actual outputs. ### 1.4 Why Use Neural Networks? Neural networks are preferred over traditional rule-based systems because they exhibit: 1. **Learning from data** — No need to explicitly program decision rules. 2. **Generalization** — They produce reasonable outputs for unseen inputs. 3. **Non-linearity** — They can model highly complex, non-linear relationships. 4. **Fault tolerance** — Graceful degradation when parts of the network are damaged. 5. **Massive parallelism** — Computations are distributed across many simple units. ### 1.5 Evolution of Neural Networks (Historical Timeline) | Year | Milestone | Contributor | |---|---|---| | 1943 | MCP Neuron Model | McCulloch & Pitts | | 1949 | Hebbian Learning Rule | Donald Hebb | | 1958 | Perceptron | Frank Rosenblatt | | 1969 | Limitations of Perceptrons (AI Winter) | Minsky & Papert | | 1982 | Hopfield Network | John Hopfield | | 1986 | Backpropagation Algorithm | Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams | | 2006 | Deep Belief Networks (Deep Learning Revival) | Hinton & Salakhutdinov | | 2012 | AlexNet wins ImageNet (CNN era) | Krizhevsky et al. | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Model of a General Artificial Neuron A modern artificial neuron (also called a **processing element** or **node**) operates in the following stages: **Step 1 — Weighted Summation (Propagation):** Each input signal $x_i$ is multiplied by its corresponding synaptic weight $w_i$, and a **bias** term $b$ is added. $$\text{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$ **Step 2 — Activation (Transformation):** The net input is passed through a non-linear **activation function** $f(\cdot)$ to produce the final output $y$. $$y = f(\text{net}) = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **The Role of Bias:** The bias $b$ acts like the intercept in linear regression. It shifts the activation function left or right, allowing the neuron to learn decision boundaries that do not necessarily pass through the origin. Without bias, the neuron is severely restricted in its representational capacity. ### 2.2 Architectural Topologies of Neural Networks Neural networks are broadly classified by the **arrangement of neurons into layers** and the **direction of signal flow**: | Architecture Type | Structural Description | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Single-Layer Feedforward** | Input layer directly connected to output layer; no hidden layers | Linear classification (e.g., original Perceptron) | | **Multi-Layer Feedforward (MLP)** | One or more hidden layers between input and output | Complex non-linear classification, function approximation | | **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** | Contains feedback loops; output fed back as input to same/previous layer | Time-series, sequence modeling, NLP | | **Self-Organizing Maps (SOM)** | Competitive learning with topological neighborhood preservation | Dimensionality reduction, clustering | | **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** | Uses convolution and pooling layers; weight sharing | Image and video processing | | **Modular Network** | Several independent networks combined via an intermediary | Large-scale complex tasks | ### 2.3 Learning Paradigms in Neural Networks | Learning Type | Description | Example Algorithm | |---|---|---| | **Supervised Learning** | Network is provided with input-output pairs (labeled data); learns to map inputs to correct outputs | Perceptron, Backpropagation | | **Unsupervised Learning** | Network discovers patterns in unlabeled data; no teacher signal | Kohonen SOM, Hebbian Learning, Autoencoders | | **Reinforcement Learning** | Network learns via interaction with environment using rewards and penalties | Q-Learning with Neural Networks | | **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Combines small labeled data with large unlabeled data | Deep belief networks | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Application | |---|---|---| | Weighted Sum (Pre-activation) | $\text{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Aggregate input signals at a neuron | | Neuron Output | $y = f(\text{net})$ | Apply non-linearity to the net input | | McCulloch-Pitts Output | $y = \mathbb{1}(\sum w_i x_i \geq \theta)$ | Binary threshold output | | Hebbian Learning Rule | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$ | Adjust weight by product of input and output | | Perceptron Update Rule | $w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + \eta (t - y) x_i$ | Correct misclassification | | Widrow-Hoff (LMS) Rule | $w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + \eta (t - y) x_i$ | Minimize squared error | | Bias as Augmented Input | $b = w_0$ with $x_0 = 1$ | Embed bias in weight vector | | Number of Parameters in MLP | $\sum (n_{\ell-1} + 1) \cdot n_\ell$ | Compute trainable parameters | | Decision Boundary Slope | $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$ | Linearly separable classification | | Vector Form of Net Input | $\text{net} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Compact matrix notation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** These foundational equations power virtually every modern AI system — from the spam filter in your email (single-layer perceptron) to the language model behind chatbots (deep multi-layer transformer networks). The bias term $b$ and the choice of activation function $f$ are critical hyperparameters that determine the network's expressiveness. ### 2.5 Why Non-Linear Activation Functions? If only linear activation functions were used, stacking multiple layers would be mathematically equivalent to a single layer — the network would lose its ability to learn complex patterns. The **Universal Approximation Theorem** states that a feedforward network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of non-linear neurons can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy. ### 2.6 Common Activation Functions (Foundation for Module 2) | Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Step (Threshold)** | $f(\text{net}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{net} \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{net} < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | Undefined at 0 | MCP Neuron, Perceptron | | **Sign (Bipolar)** | $f(\text{net}) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{net} \geq 0 \\ -1 & \text{net} < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ | Zero everywhere | Pattern classification | | **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $f(\text{net}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\text{net}}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $f(\text{net}) (1 - f(\text{net}))$ | Output layer for binary classification | | **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)** | $f(\text{net}) = \frac{e^{\text{net}} - e^{-\text{net}}}{e^{\text{net}} + e^{-\text{net}}}$ | $(-1, +1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(\text{net})$ | Hidden layers (zero-centered) | | **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)** | $f(\text{net}) = \max(0, \text{net})$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & \text{net} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{net} \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | Hidden layers in deep networks | | **Linear (Identity)** | $f(\text{net}) = \text{net}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $1$ | Output layer for regression | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Biological Neuron to Artificial Neuron **Step 1 — Biological Observation:** A biological neuron receives signals $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ through its dendrites. Each synapse has a strength (efficacy) $w_i$ that modulates the incoming signal. The soma aggregates these modulated signals. **Step 2 — Formal Aggregation:** Mathematically, this aggregation is a **weighted linear combination** of the inputs. $$\text{net} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n$$ **Step 3 — Addition of Bias:** We introduce a bias term $b$ to provide an "offset" — this allows the neuron to activate even when all inputs are zero. The augmented input is $x_0 = 1$ with weight $w_0 = b$. $$\text{net} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i x_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$$ where $\mathbf{w} = [w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_n]^T$ and $\mathbf{x} = [1, x_1, \ldots, x_n]^T$. **Step 4 — Decision / Firing:** The biological neuron either fires (action potential) or does not. We model this with an activation function $f$: $$y = f(\text{net}) = f\left(\sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i x_i\right)$$ **Step 5 — Complete MCP Neuron Equation:** For the threshold case with threshold $\theta$: $$y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - \theta\right) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \geq \theta \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Explained:** The transition from biology to mathematics involves three key reductions: (1) **quantization** — continuous signals become discrete values, (2) **linearity** — complex electrochemical integration becomes a simple weighted sum, and (3) **determinism** — stochastic firing becomes a deterministic threshold function. These simplifications enable the model to be simulated on a digital computer. ### 3.2 Worked Example: McCulloch-Pitts Implementation of AND Gate **Problem:** Show that the MCP neuron can realize the logical AND function with two inputs. **Given:** - Two inputs: $x_1, x_2 \in \{0, 1\}$ - Truth table for AND: $(0,0)\to 0$; $(0,1)\to 0$; $(1,0)\to 0$; $(1,1)\to 1$ **Solution:** We must find weights $w_1, w_2$ and threshold $\theta$ such that: $$y = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 \geq \theta \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 1:** From the first row $(0,0) \to 0$: $$w_1(0) + w_2(0) = 0 < \theta \implies \theta > 0$$ **Step 2:** From the second row $(0,1) \to 0$: $$w_1(0) + w_2(1) = w_2 < \theta \implies w_2 < \theta$$ **Step 3:** From the third row $(1,0) \to 0$: $$w_1(1) + w_2(0) = w_1 < \theta \implies w_1 < \theta$$ **Step 4:** From the fourth row $(1,1) \to 1$: $$w_1(1) + w_2(1) = w_1 + w_2 \geq \theta$$ **Step 5:** Combining constraints $w_1 < \theta$, $w_2 < \theta$, $w_1 + w_2 \geq \theta$: Choose $w_1 = w_2 = 1$ and $\theta = 1.5$. Then: - $0 < 1.5$ ✓ - $1 < 1.5$ ✓ - $1 < 1.5$ ✓ - $2 \geq 1.5$ ✓ **Final Answer:** AND gate is realized by an MCP neuron with $w_1 = w_2 = 1$, $\theta = 1.5$. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How MCP Solves Different Boolean Functions | Boolean Function | Weights $(w_1, w_2)$ | Threshold $\theta$ | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **AND** | $(1, 1)$ | $1.5$ | Output 1 only when both inputs are 1 | | **OR** | $(1, 1)$ | $0.5$ | Output 1 when at least one input is 1 | | **AND NOT** $(x_1 \text{ AND NOT } x_2)$ | $(1, -1)$ | $0.5$ | Inhibitory weight for second input | | **NOR** | $(-1, -1)$ | $-0.5$ | Both weights inhibitory | | **XOR** | **Not representable** | — | Limitation: requires non-linear decision boundary | | **NAND** | $(-1, -1)$ | $-1.5$ | Output 0 only when both are 1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Minsky-Papert Limitation (1969):** The single-layer MCP neuron *cannot* realize the XOR function because XOR is not linearly separable. This limitation triggered the first **AI Winter** and motivated the development of **multi-layer networks with hidden layers** — a key topic for Module 2 of the KTU syllabus. ### 3.4 Derivation: Hebbian Learning Rule **Donald Hebb (1949) stated:** *"When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite cell B, and repeatedly or persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells such that A's efficiency, as one of the cells firing B, is increased."* Mathematically expressed as: $$\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \, x_i \, y_j$$ where: - $\Delta w_{ij}$ = change in weight from neuron $i$ to neuron $j$ - $\eta$ = learning rate (typically $0 < \eta \leq 1$) - $x_i$ = input (activation of pre-synaptic neuron) - $y_j$ = output (activation of post-synaptic neuron) Expanded weight update: $$w_{ij}^{(\text{new})} = w_{ij}^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, x_i \, y_j$$ **Conversion Logic:** The product $x_i y_j$ means the weight increases only when both pre- and post-synaptic neurons are active simultaneously ("fire together, wire together"). If either is inactive, no learning occurs. ### 3.5 Derivation: Perceptron Learning Rule (Rosenblatt, 1958) The Perceptron generalizes Hebbian learning by incorporating an **error term** $(t - y)$ where $t$ is the target output: $$w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, (t - y) \, x_i$$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{If } t = y \text{ (correct)}: \quad & w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + 0 = w_i^{(\text{old})} \\ \text{If } t = 1, y = 0 \text{ (false negative)}: \quad & w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} + \eta \cdot x_i \\ \text{If } t = 0, y = 1 \text{ (false positive)}: \quad & w_i^{(\text{new})} = w_i^{(\text{old})} - \eta \cdot x_i \end{aligned}$$ This rule is guaranteed to converge in a finite number of steps if and only if the data is **linearly separable** (Perceptron Convergence Theorem). ### 3.6 Worked Example: Perceptron Learning for OR Gate **Initialization:** $w_1 = 0.2$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $b = 0.1$, $\eta = 0.5$, threshold activation. **Iteration 1:** Input $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$, target $t = 0$. $$\begin{aligned} \text{net} &= w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0.2(0) + 0.3(0) + 0.1 = 0.1 \\ y &= 1 \text{ (since } 0.1 \geq 0\text{)} \\ \text{Error} &= t - y = 0 - 1 = -1 \\ w_1^{\text{new}} &= 0.2 + 0.5(-1)(0) = 0.2 \\ w_2^{\text{new}} &= 0.3 + 0.5(-1)(0) = 0.3 \\ b^{\text{new}} &= 0.1 + 0.5(-1) = -0.4 \end{aligned}$$ **Iteration 2:** Input $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 0)$, target $t = 0$. $$\begin{aligned} \text{net} &= 0.2(0) + 0.3(0) + (-0.4) = -0.4 \\ y &= 0 \text{ (since } -0.4 < 0\text{)} \\ \text{Error} &= 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \text{(Converged)} \end{aligned}$$ **Iteration 3:** Input $(x_1, x_2) = (0, 1)$, target $t = 1$. $$\begin{aligned} \text{net} &= 0.2(0) + 0.3(1) + (-0.4) = -0.1 \\ y &= 0 \quad \text{(incorrect)} \\ \text{Error} &= 1 - 0 = 1 \\ w_1^{\text{new}} &= 0.2 + 0.5(1)(0) = 0.2 \\ w_2^{\text{new}} &= 0.3 + 0.5(1)(1) = 0.8 \\ b^{\text{new}} &= -0.4 + 0.5(1) = 0.1 \end{aligned}$$ This process continues until all four training patterns are correctly classified. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Biological Neuron vs. Artificial Neuron ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Bio["BIOLOGICAL NEURON"] A1["Input Signals"] --> B1["Dendrites
Reception"] B1 --> C1["Soma
Cell Body
Integration"] C1 --> D1["Axon Hillock
Threshold Check"] D1 -->|"Fires"| E1["Axon
Output Signal"] E1 --> F1["Synapse
Strength Modulation"] end subgraph Art["ARTIFICIAL NEURON"] A2["Inputs x1..xn"] --> B2["Synaptic
Weights w1..wn"] B2 --> C2["Summing
Junction"] C2 --> D2["Activation
Function f net"] D2 --> E2["Output y"] end classDef bioStyle fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef artStyle fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,F1 bioStyle class A2,B2,C2,D2,E2 artStyle ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Architecture: Multi-Layer Feedforward Neural Network (MLP) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputL["INPUT LAYER L0"] I1["x1"]:::inputNode I2["x2"]:::inputNode I3["x3"]:::inputNode I4["x4"]:::inputNode end subgraph HiddenL["HIDDEN LAYER L1"] H1["h1 = f net1"]:::hiddenNode H2["h2 = f net2"]:::hiddenNode H3["h3 = f net3"]:::hiddenNode end subgraph HiddenL2["HIDDEN LAYER L2"] K1["k1 = f net4"]:::hiddenNode K2["k2 = f net5"]:::hiddenNode end subgraph OutputL["OUTPUT LAYER L3"] O1["y1"]:::outputNode O2["y2"]:::outputNode end I1 --> H1 I1 --> H2 I1 --> H3 I2 --> H1 I2 --> H2 I2 --> H3 I3 --> H1 I3 --> H2 I3 --> H3 I4 --> H1 I4 --> H2 I4 --> H3 H1 --> K1 H1 --> K2 H2 --> K1 H2 --> K2 H3 --> K1 H3 --> K2 K1 --> O1 K1 --> O2 K2 --> O1 K2 --> O2 classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef hiddenNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow: Neural Network Training Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Initialize Weights and Biases
Random small values"] --> B["Present Input Pattern x1..xn
from Training Set"] B --> C["Compute Net Input
net = w transposed x + b"] C --> D["Apply Activation Function
y = f net"] D --> E["Compare with Target
Compute Error e = t minus y"] E --> F{"Error Within
Tolerance?"} F -->|Yes| G["STOP Training
Converged"] F -->|No| H["Update Weights
w new = w old plus eta times e times x"] H --> I["Update Bias
b new = b old plus eta times e"] I --> B classDef startNode fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 classDef computeNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 class A startNode class C,D,E,H,I computeNode class F decisionNode class G endNode class B startNode ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Single-Layer vs. Multi-Layer Network ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SLN["SINGLE LAYER PERCEPTRON"] direction LR SLI1["x1"] --> SLO["y = f sum wi xi"] SLI2["x2"] --> SLO SLI3["x3"] --> SLO SLN_note["Only linearly separable problems
Example: AND OR NAND"]:::noteStyle end subgraph MLN["MULTI LAYER PERCEPTRON MLP"] direction LR MLI1["x1"] --> MLH1["h1"] --> MLO1["y1"] MLI2["x2"] --> MLH1 MLI3["x3"] --> MLH2["h2"] --> MLO1 MLI1 --> MLH2 MLI2 --> MLH3["h3"] --> MLO2["y2"] MLI3 --> MLH3 MLH1 --> MLO2 MLH2 --> MLO2 MLN_note["Non linearly separable problems
Example: XOR parity checker"]:::noteStyle end classDef noteStyle fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666,color:#000 ``` ### 4.5 Concept Map: Learning Paradigms in Neural Networks ```mermaid flowchart TD L["Neural Network
Learning Paradigms"]:::rootNode L --> SUP["Supervised Learning"]:::supNode L --> UNS["Unsupervised Learning"]:::unsNode L --> REI["Reinforcement Learning"]:::reiNode L --> SEM["Semi Supervised Learning"]:::semNode SUP --> SUP1["Perceptron Learning"]:::leafNode SUP --> SUP2["Backpropagation"]:::leafNode SUP --> SUP3["RBF Networks"]:::leafNode UNS --> UNS1["Hebbian Learning"]:::leafNode UNS --> UNS2["Kohonen SOM"]:::leafNode UNS --> UNS3["Autoencoders"]:::leafNode REI --> REI1["Q Learning"]:::leafNode REI --> REI2["Actor Critic Methods"]:::leafNode SEM --> SEM1["Deep Belief Networks"]:::leafNode SEM --> SEM2["Generative Adversarial Networks"]:::leafNode classDef rootNode fill:#343a40,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef supNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef unsNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef reiNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef semNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef leafNode fill:#ffffff,stroke:#666,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Define an Artificial Neural Network. Draw the architecture of a biological neuron and list its three main components.** **Model Answer:** An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a parallel, distributed information processing system inspired by the biological nervous system. It consists of interconnected processing elements (artificial neurons) that process information through their connection weights in response to external inputs. The three main components of a biological neuron are: 1. **Dendrites** — receive input signals from other neurons 2. **Soma (Cell Body)** — integrates the incoming signals 3. **Axon** — transmits the output signal to other neurons via **synapses** *[Diagram of biological neuron should be drawn — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for components]* --- #### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Compare the McCulloch-Pitts neuron model and the Perceptron model.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | McCulloch-Pitts (1943) | Perceptron (1958) | |---|---|---| | Weights | Fixed, non-adaptive | Adjustable via learning rule | | Learning capability | None (logic gate realization only) | Yes (learns from examples) | | Activation function | Binary threshold | Binary threshold (linear) | | Input values | Binary $\{0, 1\}$ | Real-valued (typically) | | Bias/Threshold | External threshold $\theta$ | Can be absorbed as a weight $w_0$ | | Applications | Implementing Boolean functions | Binary classification, pattern recognition | | Limitation | Cannot learn; weights are hand-crafted | Cannot solve non-linearly separable problems (e.g., XOR) | --- ### 📕 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part B questions, students commonly lose marks by: (1) drawing incomplete diagrams without labeling weights, biases, and activation functions; (2) forgetting to include the **bias term** in the net input computation; (3) not showing the iteration table step-by-step in perceptron learning problems; (4) missing the **activation function** in the model diagram; (5) confusing the McCulloch-Pitts update rule (no learning) with the Perceptron update rule (error-based learning). Always label all components clearly and present your work in a structured tabular format. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain the McCulloch-Pitts (MCP) neuron model with a neat labeled diagram. Using this model, show how an **AND gate** and an **OR gate** can be realized. (7 marks) **(b)** What is a **Perceptron**? Draw its architecture. Explain the **Perceptron learning algorithm** with a suitable example. Discuss the **limitations of the single-layer perceptron**. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution (a):** **Definition:** The McCulloch-Pitts (MCP) neuron (1943) is the earliest computational model of an artificial neuron. It is a binary threshold logic unit. **Architecture (Textual Block Diagram):** The MCP neuron receives $n$ binary inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \in \{0, 1\}$. Each input is multiplied by a fixed weight $w_i$. The summing junction aggregates them and compares the sum against a threshold $\theta$. The output is $y = 1$ if the sum $\geq \theta$, else $y = 0$. $$\text{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i, \quad y = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{net} \geq \theta \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Truth Table for AND Gate:** | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Target $t$ | $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2$ | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | $w_1(0) + w_2(0) = 0$ | $0 < 1.5$ → $y = 0$ ✓ | | 0 | 1 | 0 | $w_1(0) + w_2(1) = w_2$ | $w_2 < 1.5$ → $y = 0$ ✓ | | 1 | 0 | 0 | $w_1(1) + w_2(0) = w_1$ | $w_1 < 1.5$ → $y = 0$ ✓ | | 1 | 1 | 1 | $w_1(1) + w_2(1) = w_1 + w_2$ | $w_1 + w_2 \geq 1.5$ → $y = 1$ ✓ | **Chosen Parameters:** $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 1$, $\theta = 1.5$. **Truth Table for OR Gate:** | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Target $t$ | $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2$ | Result | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | $0$ | $0 < 0.5$ → $y = 0$ ✓ | | 0 | 1 | 1 | $w_2 = 1$ | $1 \geq 0.5$ → $y = 1$ ✓ | | 1 | 0 | 1 | $w_1 = 1$ | $1 \geq 0.5$ → $y = 1$ ✓ | | 1 | 1 | 1 | $w_1 + w_2 = 2$ | $2 \geq 0.5$ → $y = 1$ ✓ | **Chosen Parameters:** $w_1 = 1$, $w_2 = 1$, $\theta = 0.5$. --- ### **Model Solution (b):** **Definition of Perceptron:** A Perceptron, developed by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958, is a linear binary classifier that learns the optimal weights from training data using a supervised error-correction learning rule. **Architecture:** The single-layer perceptron has an input layer (with bias) directly connected to an output neuron. The output is: $$y = f\left(\sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i x_i\right) = f(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x})$$ where $x_0 = 1$ and $w_0 = b$ is the bias. **Perceptron Learning Algorithm:** **Step 1:** Initialize weights $w_i$ to small random values (e.g., $w_i = 0$). **Step 2:** Set learning rate $\eta$ (e.g., $\eta = 0.5$). **Step 3:** For each training pair $(\mathbf{x}, t)$: -
