# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Neural Network Applications

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **full landscape of NN applications** organized by data modality and task type. This is the diagram examiners expect students to draw or describe in 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[NN Applications Taxonomy] --> rootB[Data Modality]
    rootA --> rootC[Task Type]

    rootB --> mod1[Tabular Data]
    rootB --> mod2[Image Data]
    rootB --> mod3[Sequential Data]
    rootB --> mod4[Audio Speech Data]
    rootB --> mod5[Graph Structured Data]

    mod1 --> app1A[Medical Diagnosis]
    mod1 --> app1B[Credit Scoring]
    mod1 --> app1C[Customer Churn Prediction]

    mod2 --> app2A[Object Detection]
    mod2 --> app2B[Face Recognition]
    mod2 --> app2C[Medical Imaging Tumor Detection]
    mod2 --> app2D[OCR Text Extraction]
    mod2 --> app2E[Satellite Image Analysis]

    mod3 --> app3A[Stock Price Forecasting]
    mod3 --> app3B[Weather Prediction]
    mod3 --> app3C[Power Load Forecasting]
    mod3 --> app3D[Language Translation]
    mod3 --> app3E[Anomaly Detection Time Series]

    mod4 --> app4A[Speech Recognition]
    mod4 --> app4B[Speaker Identification]
    mod4 --> app4C[Music Genre Classification]
    mod4 --> app4D[Emotion Detection from Voice]

    mod5 --> app5A[Social Network Analysis]
    mod5 --> app5B[Molecular Property Prediction]
    mod5 --> app5C[Recommendation Systems]

    rootC --> task1[Classification]
    rootC --> task2[Regression]
    rootC --> task3[Clustering]
    rootC --> task4[Control]
    rootC --> task5[Generation]

    task1 --> net1[MLP CNN RNN]
    task2 --> net2[MLP LSTM GRU]
    task3 --> net3[SOM Autoencoder]
    task4 --> net4[DQN DDPG Actor Critic]
    task5 --> net5[GAN VAE Diffusion Model]
```

## 4.2 Application Selection Decision Flowchart

This block diagram shows **how to choose the right network** for a given application — a frequently asked KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Problem] --> q1{Is data labelled?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Task discrete or continuous?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Goal discover structure or generate?}
    q2 -- Discrete Classes --> q4{Sequential or spatial?}
    q2 -- Continuous Value --> q5{Sequential or static?}
    q3 -- Discover Structure --> q6[Use SOM or Autoencoder]
    q3 -- Generate New Samples --> q7[Use GAN or VAE]
    q4 -- Yes --> q8[Use CNN for images]
    q4 -- No --> q9[Use RNN LSTM for sequences]
    q4 -- Tabular --> q10[Use MLP]
    q5 -- Yes --> q11[Use LSTM or GRU]
    q5 -- No --> q12[Use MLP for regression]
    q8 --> q13[Output Softmax plus Cross Entropy]
    q9 --> q14[Output Softmax per timestep]
    q10 --> q15[Output Sigmoid or Softmax]
    q11 --> q16[Output Linear plus MSE]
    q12 --> q17[Output Linear plus MSE]
    q6 --> q18[Output Cluster Index Map]
    q7 --> q19[Output Generated Sample]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Typical NN Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blk1[Raw Data Source] --> blk2[Preprocessing Module]
    blk2 --> blk3[Feature Extraction Module]

    blk1 --> doc1[Sensors Images Text Audio Tabular]
    blk2 --> doc2[Normalize Denoise Resize Tokenize]
    blk3 --> doc3[PCA CNN Embedding Statistical Features]

    blk5 --> doc5[Softmax Sigmoid Argmax Linear]

    blk7 --> doc7[Class Label Numeric Forecast Action]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List any six real-world applications of artificial neural networks with a one-line description of each."**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**

| # | Application | One-Line Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Handwritten character recognition** | MLPs/CNNs classify digits/letters from images (e.g., MNIST, postal-code reading) |
| 2 | **Speech recognition** | RNNs/LSTMs transcribe spoken words into text (e.g., Google Voice, Siri) |
| 3 | **Medical diagnosis** | MLPs classify diseases from clinical features (e.g., diabetes, cancer detection) |
| 4 | **Stock market prediction** | LSTMs forecast future prices from historical time-series data |
| 5 | **Image classification** | CNNs assign labels to images (e.g., cat vs. dog, satellite imagery) |
| 6 | **Fraud detection in banking** | Autoencoders flag anomalous transactions by high reconstruction error |

*(Any six applications with valid descriptions fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Why are neural networks considered universal function approximators? State the theorem."**

**Model Answer:**
According to the **Universal Approximation Theorem** (Cybenko 1989, Hornik 1991):

> A feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons, using any standard squashing activation function (e.g., sigmoid), can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to an arbitrary degree of accuracy.

Mathematically, for any continuous $f: [0,1]^n \to \mathbb{R}$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, there exist weights $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{b}$ and a finite $N$ such that:

$$
\mid f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \, \sigma(\mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b_i) \mid < \epsilon
$$

**Significance for applications:** Any well-defined input-output mapping — whether predicting cancer, recognizing faces, or controlling a robot — can, in principle, be learned by a sufficiently wide MLP. *(Full 3 marks for stating the theorem, writing the equation, and giving the significance.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Either attempt **OR** (full Module 1 question) or both sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern]

**"Explain in detail the various application areas of artificial neural networks. Discuss the role of different network architectures (MLP, CNN, RNN) in solving classification, regression, and sequence-prediction problems."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Discuss the major application domains of ANNs with at least one example from each domain."**

**Model Answer:**

Neural network applications span virtually every engineering and scientific discipline. The major domains are:

**1. Pattern Classification**
- **Example:** Handwritten digit recognition on the MNIST dataset using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).
- **Input:** A flattened $28 \times 28$ grayscale image → 784-dimensional vector $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Output:** A 10-dimensional one-hot vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ via Softmax activation.
- **Loss:** Categorical Cross-Entropy: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{10} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$.
- **Achievement:** State-of-the-art MLPs reach **> 99% accuracy** on MNIST.

**2. Function Approximation / Regression**
- **Example:** Predicting house prices from features (area, bedrooms, location).
- **Network:** MLP with a single linear-output neuron.
- **Loss:** MSE: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.

**3. Time-Series Prediction**
- **Example:** Forecasting daily electricity demand.
- **Network:** LSTM that processes the past 30 days of consumption and outputs day 31.
- **Justification:** LSTMs use forget/input/output gates to retain long-term seasonal patterns (e.g., higher AC demand in summer).

**4. Image Processing**
- **Example:** Tumor detection in MRI scans.
- **Network:** Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with successive Conv → ReLU → Pool layers, terminating in a fully-connected Sigmoid output.
- **Justification:** Convolutional filters detect local features (edges, textures); pooling provides translation invariance.

**5. Speech and Natural Language Processing**
- **Example:** Voice-to-text transcription.
- **Network:** Deep RNN or LSTM with Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss for unaligned outputs.

**6. Control and Robotics**
- **Example:** Inverted pendulum balancing.
- **Network:** MLP trained via reinforcement learning (Q-learning or policy gradient) to output control actions given the pendulum's angle and angular velocity.

**7. Clustering and Feature Mapping**
- **Example:** Customer segmentation from purchase history.
- **Network:** Self-Organizing Map (SOM) — an unsupervised competitive network that produces a 2D topology-preserving map.

**8. Anomaly Detection**
- **Example:** Credit-card fraud detection.
- **Network:** Autoencoder trained only on legitimate transactions; fraud is flagged when reconstruction error exceeds a threshold.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Compare the suitability of MLP, CNN, and RNN for different application types. Provide a comparison table and explain the architectural reasoning."**

**Model Answer:**

The choice of network is dictated by **data structure**, not by data size alone.

**Architectural Reasoning:**

- **MLP (Multi-Layer Perceptron):** Composed of fully-connected layers. Each neuron in layer $\ell$ is connected to **every** neuron in layer $\ell - 1$.
  - **Strengths:** Simple; works for tabular data with low-to-moderate dimensionality.
  - **Weaknesses:** No spatial or temporal awareness. A $256 \times 256$ image would require $65{,}536$ input neurons — computationally expensive and prone to overfitting.
  - **Best applications:** Medical diagnosis, credit scoring, simple regression on structured data.

- **CNN (Convolutional Neural Network):** Uses **convolutional layers** with shared weights (kernels) and **pooling layers** for downsampling.
  - **Strengths:** Exploits **spatial locality** and **translation invariance**; drastically fewer parameters than a fully-connected network.
  - **Weaknesses:** Inherently static; not designed for sequential or temporal data.
  - **Best applications:** Image classification, object detection, facial recognition, medical imaging.

- **RNN / LSTM (Recurrent Neural Network):** Maintains a **hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_t$ that is updated at each time step using both the current input $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the previous state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$.
  - **Strengths:** Captures **temporal dependencies** of arbitrary length. LSTM variants solve the vanishing-gradient problem using gated memory cells.
  - **Weaknesses:** Sequential computation is hard to parallelize; training is slower than CNNs.
  - **Best applications:** Language modeling, machine translation, speech recognition, stock prediction.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | MLP | CNN | RNN / LSTM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Tabular, low-dim vectors | 2D / 3D images | Sequences, time series, text |
| **Spatial awareness** | None | Yes (local receptive fields) | None (but temporal awareness) |
| **Temporal awareness** | None | None | Yes (via recurrent connections) |
| **Parameter count** | High (fully connected) | Low (weight sharing) | Moderate (shared across time) |
| **Training algorithm** | Backpropagation | Backpropagation | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) |
| **Typical application** | Medical diagnosis | Image classification | Language translation |
| **Activation in final layer** | Sigmoid / Softmax / Linear | Softmax / Sigmoid | Softmax (per step) / Linear |
| **Loss function** | Cross-Entropy / MSE | Cross-Entropy | CTC / Cross-Entropy / MSE |

**Conclusion:** The application drives the architecture. Engineers must always ask: *Is my data tabular, spatial, or sequential?* The answer uniquely determines the appropriate NN family.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**"Describe the architecture and working of a Self-Organizing Map (SOM). Explain with a suitable example how SOMs are used in customer segmentation. How is the Best Matching Unit (BMU) determined and updated?"**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Describe the architecture of a SOM and the competitive learning algorithm used to train it."**

**Model Answer:**

A **Self-Organizing Map (SOM)**, introduced by Teuvo Kohonen (1982), is an **unsupervised** competitive neural network that maps high-dimensional input data onto a low-dimensional (typically 2D) **topology-preserving grid**.

**Architecture:**
- A 2D lattice (often rectangular or hexagonal) of $M \times N$ neurons.
- Each neuron $j$ has a weight vector $\mathbf{w}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d$ is the input dimensionality.
- **No output layer**; the "output" is the spatial location of the winning neuron.

**Competitive Learning Algorithm:**

1. **Initialize** all weight vectors $\mathbf{w}_j(0)$ with small random values.
2. **For each input sample** $\mathbf{x}(t)$:
   - **Step 1 — Find the BMU** $c$:

   $$
   c = \arg\min_{j} \mid\mid \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t) \mid\mid
   $$

   - **Step 2 — Update the BMU and its neighbors:**

   $$
   \mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + \alpha(t) \cdot h_{cj}(t) \cdot [\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)]
   $$

   where:
   - $\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 \exp(-t / \tau_\alpha)$ is the **decaying learning rate**.
   - $h_{cj}(t) = \exp(-d_{cj}^2 / 2\sigma^2(t))$ is the **Gaussian neighborhood function**.
   - $d_{cj}$ is the **lattice distance** between neurons $c$ and $j$.

3. **Reduce** both $\alpha$ and $\sigma$ gradually over epochs.
4. **Repeat** until convergence.

**Key Property — Topology Preservation:** Neurons that are **spatially close on the grid** respond to **similar inputs**. This means the 2D map is a meaningful low-dimensional representation of the high-dimensional input space.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Explain with a numerical example how a SOM can be used for customer segmentation based on two features: Age and Annual Income."**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup:**
- Input dimension $d = 2$ (features: Age in years, Annual Income in $10^3$ ₹).
- SOM grid: $1 \times 3$ (3 neurons in a row, for simplicity).
- Initial weight vectors: $\mathbf{w}_1 = [25, 30]$, $\mathbf{w}_2 = [40, 50]$, $\mathbf{w}_3 = [60, 70]$.
- Learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$, neighborhood radius $\sigma = 1$ (all neurons are neighbors for the $1 \times 3$ grid).
- A new customer arrives with $\mathbf{x} = [35, 45]$.

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances:**

$$
d_1 = \sqrt{(35 - 25)^2 + (45 - 30)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 225} = \sqrt{325} \approx 18.03
$$

$$
d_2 = \sqrt{(35 - 40)^2 + (45 - 50)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07
$$

$$
d_3 = \sqrt{(35 - 60)^2 + (45 - 70)^2} = \sqrt{625 + 625} = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36
$$

**Step 2 — Identify BMU:** $c = 2$ (smallest distance).

**Step 3 — Compute neighborhood coefficients** (with $d_{c,j}$ being the lattice distance, i.e., number of neurons between $c$ and $j$):
- $h_{c,1} = \exp(-1^2 / 2 \cdot 1^2) = \exp(-0.5) = 0.6065$
- $h_{c,2} = \exp(0) = 1.0000$ (BMU itself)
- $h_{c,3} = \exp(-0.5) = 0.6065$

**Step 4 — Update weights:**

For neuron 1:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1^{\text{new}} = [25, 30] + 0.5 \times 0.6065 \times ([35, 45] - [25, 30])
$$

$$
= [25, 30] + 0.3033 \times [10, 15] = [25 + 3.033, 30 + 4.549] = [28.03, 34.55]
$$

For neuron 2 (BMU):

$$
\mathbf{w}_2^{\text{new}} = [40, 50] + 0.5 \times 1.0 \times ([35, 45] - [40, 50])
$$

$$
= [40, 50] + 0.5 \times [-5, -5] = [40 - 2.5, 50 - 2.5] = [37.50, 47.50]
$$

For neuron 3:

$$
\mathbf{w}_3^{\text{new}} = [60, 70] + 0.5 \times 0.6065 \times ([35, 45] - [60, 70])
$$

$$
= [60, 70] + 0.3033 \times [-25, -25] = [60 - 7.583, 70 - 7.583] = [52.42, 62.42]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The BMU (neuron 2) moved **most strongly** toward the input — it now represents the "middle-aged, mid-income" customer segment. Neighboring neurons 1 and 3 also shifted, but less. After thousands of such updates, each of the 3 neurons will specialize in one of 3 natural customer segments: *Young & Low-Income, Middle-Aged & Mid-Income, Senior & High-Income*.

**Application Impact:** A marketing team can then target each neuron (segment) with tailored campaigns — a clear, real-world deployment of unsupervised neural learning.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in "Applications of NN" questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Conflating network types:** Writing "CNN is used for stock prediction" or "RNN is used for image classification" — these are *domain-architecture mismatches*. Always justify the pairing.
> 2. **❌ Skipping the loss-function discussion:** Board examiners expect you to state the loss function for any application. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **❌ Forgetting the activation function at the output:** Sigmoid for binary, Softmax for multi-class, Linear for regression. Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **❌ No justification in comparison tables:** Just listing "MLP / CNN / RNN" without explaining *why* each is suited to its application loses marks.
> 5. **❌ Ignoring the Universal Approximation Theorem:** When asked "why NNs work for applications," many students skip this theorem entirely. It is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 6. **❌ Missing equations in numerical SOM questions:** Students frequently forget the BMU formula and the update rule. These are worth 3–4 marks combined.
> 7. **❌ Not mentioning preprocessing:** Real applications always require normalization, denoising, or tokenization. Mentioning it shows engineering maturity.
> 8. **❌ Treating "Applications" as a memorization-only topic:** Examiners reward the *mapping logic* (data type → task → network → loss → activation). A generic list without the mapping logic scores poorly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Applications of Neural Networks**

- **Core idea:** A neural network is a **universal approximator** of continuous functions — this single property underlies all its applications.
- **Five-stage application pipeline:** Problem formulation → Data preprocessing → Architecture selection → Training → Inference.
- **Domain–Architecture Mapping (must memorize):**
  - Tabular, low-dim → **MLP**
  - Image / spatial → **CNN**
  - Sequence / temporal → **RNN / LSTM / GRU**
  - Unsupervised clustering → **SOM / Autoencoder**
  - Reinforcement (game, control) → **DQN / DDPG**
  - Generation → **GAN / VAE**
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** Single hidden-layer MLP with sigmoid can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary $\epsilon$-accuracy.
- **Output Activation Cheat Sheet:** Binary classification → Sigmoid; Multi-class → Softmax; Regression → Linear; Hidden layers → ReLU (default).
- **Loss Function Cheat Sheet:** Binary classification → Binary Cross-Entropy; Multi-class → Categorical Cross-Entropy; Regression → MSE; Sequence-to-sequence → CTC; Reinforcement → Q-Learning / Policy Gradient loss.
- **Standard Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score (for classification); MSE, RMSE, MAE (for regression); Quantization Error (for SOMs).
- **SOM Key Equations:** BMU = $\arg\min_j \mid\mid \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \mid\mid$; Update: $\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + \alpha(t) h_{cj}(t)[\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t)]$.
- **CNN Key Operation:** Convolution $S(i,j) = \sum_m \sum_n K(m,n) \cdot I(i - m, j - n)$.
- **LSTM Key Equations:** Forget gate $f_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$; Cell state $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- **Eight Major Application Domains:** Pattern classification, function approximation, time-series forecasting, image processing, speech/NLP, control & robotics, clustering, anomaly detection.
- **Engineering Field Deployments:** Biomedical (tumor detection), Civil (structural health monitoring), Mechanical (fault diagnosis), Electrical (smart-grid load forecasting), Finance (credit scoring), Aerospace (flight control).
- **Examiner's Golden Pattern:** Every application answer should follow — **Domain → Task Type → Network Architecture → Output Activation → Loss Function → Justification**.
- **The one-sentence essence:** *Neural networks are application-agnostic function approximators; the data modality dictates the architecture, and the task type dictates the loss.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Learning Methods - Supervised, Unsupervised and Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT522 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Learning Methods - Supervised, Unsupervised and Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Learning Methods in Artificial Neural Networks — The Foundation

In the context of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), **learning** refers to the algorithmic process by which a neural network's **weights, biases, and architectural parameters** are progressively adjusted so that the network can perform a desired task on given data. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECAT522, Module 1)**, the three principal paradigms of learning are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *learning method* (or *learning paradigm*) is a mathematically defined procedure that dictates how a neural network uses its interaction with an external source of information—called the *environment* or *teacher*—to modify its internal free parameters in order to minimize a pre-defined error or to maximize a cumulative reward signal.

The three canonical paradigms are:

1. **Supervised Learning** — Learning from a *teacher* using labeled input–output pairs.
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — Learning *without* a teacher, by discovering latent structure in data.
3. **Reinforcement Learning** — Learning by *trial-and-error interaction* with an environment, guided by reward and punishment signals.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three "Teachers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Three Students, Three Schools**
>
> Imagine three students learning to identify fruits:
>
> | Student | Type of Learning | Analogy |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Student A** | *Supervised* | Studies with a notebook of fruit images and their names ("Apple", "Mango"). A teacher checks every answer. |
> | **Student B** | *Unsupervised* | Given a huge basket of mixed fruits with **no labels**. The student groups them by similarity: "red round things together, yellow long things together." |
> | **Student C** | *Reinforcement* | Placed in an orchard. Tries a fruit → if sweet, gets a "reward" 😊; if sour, gets a "punishment" 🙁. Learns over time which trees to pick from. |

This single analogy captures the essence of all three paradigms. Now, let us formalize each.

---

## 1.3 Supervised Learning — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervised Learning** is a learning paradigm in which the network is provided with a set of training examples in the form of *labeled pairs* $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the input vector and $y^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ is the corresponding desired output (label/target). The network learns a mapping function $f_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a *loss function* $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ between predicted output $\hat{y} = f_{\theta}(x)$ and true label $y$.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Classification** — discrete output labels (e.g., spam vs. not-spam)
- **Regression** — continuous output values (e.g., house price prediction)

**Representative Algorithms:** Perceptron, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with Backpropagation, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Decision Trees, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN).

**Engineering Applications:** Image recognition, medical diagnosis, speech-to-text, fraud detection, autonomous vehicle perception.

---

## 1.4 Unsupervised Learning — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a learning paradigm in which the network is provided with *unlabeled* training data $\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ (no target output $y$ is given). The network must autonomously discover *patterns, clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or statistical regularities* inherent in the input distribution $p(x)$.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Clustering** — partitioning data into groups (e.g., customer segmentation)
- **Dimensionality Reduction** — projecting high-dimensional data to lower dimensions (e.g., PCA, t-SNE)
- **Density Estimation** — modeling the probability distribution $p(x)$
- **Feature Learning** — learning meaningful representations (e.g., autoencoders)

**Representative Algorithms:** K-Means, Hierarchical Clustering, DBSCAN, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBM), Self-Organizing Maps (SOM), Autoencoders, Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN).

**Engineering Applications:** Anomaly detection in IoT sensor streams, recommender systems, genomics data exploration, customer behavior analytics, data compression.

---

## 1.5 Reinforcement Learning — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a learning paradigm in which an *agent* learns an optimal *policy* $\pi(a \vert s)$ by interacting with a dynamic *environment*. At each time step $t$, the agent observes a *state* $s_t$, takes an *action* $a_t$, transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$, and receives a scalar *reward* $r_{t+1}$. The objective is to maximize the expected cumulative discounted reward, called the *return*.

**Core Components:**
- **Agent** — the decision-maker (the neural network policy)
- **Environment** — the external system the agent interacts with
- **State** $s_t$ — representation of the current situation
- **Action** $a_t$ — choice made by the agent
- **Reward** $r_t$ — scalar feedback signal
- **Policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ — strategy mapping states to actions
- **Value Function** — expected future reward from a state
- **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** — the formal mathematical framework

**Representative Algorithms:** Q-Learning, SARSA, Deep Q-Network (DQN), Policy Gradient methods (REINFORCE), Actor-Critic methods (A2C, A3C), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).

**Engineering Applications:** Robotics, game playing (AlphaGo, AlphaZero), autonomous driving, industrial process control, smart grid optimization, dialogue systems.

---

## 1.6 Tabular Overview of the Three Paradigms

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Provided** | Labeled $(x, y)$ pairs | Unlabeled $x$ only | State–reward signals |
| **Feedback Type** | Direct (correct answer) | None | Scalar reward (delayed) |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover structure in $p(x)$ | Maximize $G_t = \sum \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ |
| **Key Challenge** | Generalization, overfitting | Evaluation without ground truth | Exploration vs. exploitation |
| **Typical Output** | Classifier / Regressor | Clusters / Lower-dim. embedding | Policy $\pi$ or Value function |
| **Real-World Analogy** | Teacher + textbook | Self-study grouping | Trial-and-error in an orchard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Mathematical Setup

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ sampled i.i.d. from an unknown joint distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x, y)$, the network with parameters $\theta = \{W, b\}$ produces predictions:

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = f_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$$

The **empirical risk** (training loss) is:

$$\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(y^{(i)}, \hat{y}^{(i)}\big) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a per-sample loss, $\Omega(\theta)$ is a regularizer (e.g., $\ell_2$ weight decay), and $\lambda$ is the regularization strength.

### 2.1.2 The Training Loop — Step by Step

1. **Initialize** weights $W$ and biases $b$ (e.g., He initialization, Xavier initialization).
2. **Forward Pass**: Compute $\hat{y} = f_{\theta}(x)$.
3. **Compute Loss**: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$.
4. **Backward Pass**: Compute gradients $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ via backpropagation.
5. **Update Parameters** using an optimizer (e.g., Gradient Descent, Adam, RMSprop).
6. **Repeat** for multiple epochs until convergence.

### 2.1.3 Common Loss Functions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must know the following loss functions and when to apply them.

- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — used for regression:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^{2}$$

- **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** — used for binary classification:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

- **Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE)** — used for multi-class classification:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{c}^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_{c}^{(i)}$$

- **Hinge Loss** — used for SVMs:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max\!\left(0, 1 - y^{(i)} \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)$$

### 2.1.4 Why Supervised Learning Works — The "How"

The **universal approximation theorem** states that a feedforward network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate *any* continuous function on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to arbitrary accuracy. Backpropagation uses the chain rule of calculus to efficiently compute gradients of the loss with respect to every weight in the network, making training tractable even for deep architectures.

---

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Mathematical Setup

Given *unlabeled* data $\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn from an unknown distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$, the objective is to learn either:
- A **generative model** $p_{\theta}(x)$ approximating $p_{\text{data}}(x)$, or
- A **lower-dimensional representation** $z = g_{\theta}(x)$ with $z \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $m < d$, or
- A **cluster assignment function** $c: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$.

### 2.2.2 K-Means Clustering Objective

The K-Means algorithm minimizes the **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:

$$J_{\text{K-Means}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} \,\left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_{k} \right\|_{2}^{2}$$

where $r_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ is an indicator (1 if $x^{(i)}$ is assigned to cluster $k$), and $\mu_{k}$ is the centroid of cluster $k$.

### 2.2.3 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

PCA finds orthogonal directions (principal components) that maximize variance. Given centered data $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ with covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} X^{\top} X$, PCA solves:

$$\max_{w :\, \vert w \vert = 1} \quad w^{\top} \Sigma w$$

The solution is the eigenvector of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue. The projection onto the top-$m$ components is $Z = X W_{m}$.

### 2.2.4 Autoencoders

An autoencoder learns the identity mapping through a bottleneck: encoder $z = f_{\theta}(x)$, decoder $\hat{x} = g_{\phi}(z)$, trained to minimize reconstruction loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{AE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| x^{(i)} - g_{\phi}\!\left(f_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)\right) \right\|_{2}^{2}$$

The bottleneck $z$ (latent code) acts as a compressed representation.

### 2.2.5 The "Why" of Unsupervised Learning

In real engineering applications, **labels are expensive** (e.g., medical imaging requires expert radiologists). Unsupervised methods can:
- Pre-train networks for downstream supervised tasks (transfer learning).
- Detect anomalies by identifying low-density regions.
- Visualize high-dimensional data in 2D/3D.
- Discover hidden customer segments in marketing analytics.

---

## 2.3 Reinforcement Learning — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP)

An MDP is the formal tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = set of states
- $\mathcal{A}$ = set of actions
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$ = state transition probability
- $R(s, a, s')$ = reward function
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ = discount factor

The **Markov property** states: $P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \vert s_t, a_t)$.

### 2.3.2 Return, Policy, and Value Functions

The **return** (cumulative discounted reward) from time $t$ is:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$$

The **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ is a probability distribution over actions given state.

The **state-value function**:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right]$$

The **action-value function** (Q-function):

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_t \,\big\vert\, s_t = s,\, a_t = a \right]$$

### 2.3.3 The Bellman Equations (Foundational)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** The Bellman equation is the single most important equation in RL and a frequent exam question.

The Bellman equation expresses value recursively:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q^{\pi}(s', a') \right]$$

The **Bellman optimality equation**:

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{*}(s') \right]$$

### 2.3.4 Exploration vs. Exploitation

A central challenge in RL is the **exploration–exploitation dilemma**:
- **Exploitation**: Choose the action currently believed to yield the highest reward.
- **Exploration**: Try novel actions to discover potentially better strategies.

Common strategies: $\varepsilon$-greedy, Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), Boltzmann exploration.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations most frequently tested in KTU Module 1 questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supervised Loss (MSE) | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y - \hat{y})^{2}$ | Regression training |
| 2 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y \log \hat{y}$ | Classification training |
| 3 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$ | All gradient-based learning |
| 4 | Perceptron Update | $w \leftarrow w + \eta \, y^{(i)} x^{(i)}$ | Linear classifier |
| 5 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i}\sum_{k} r_{ik}\vert\vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{k}\vert\vert^{2}$ | Clustering |
| 6 | PCA Objective | $\max_{w} w^{\top}\Sigma w$ s.t. $\vert w\vert = 1$ | Dimensionality reduction |
| 7 | Return (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative future reward |
| 8 | State-Value Function | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t = s]$ | Policy evaluation |
| 9 | Action-Value Function | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t=s, a_t=a]$ | Action selection |
| 10 | Bellman Equation | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Recursive value |
| 11 | Bellman Optimality | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$ | Optimal policy |
| 12 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Off-policy TD learning |
| 13 | Discount Factor Constraint | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ | Ensures finite return |
| 14 | Autoencoder Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \vert\vert x - g(f(x))\vert\vert^{2}$ | Reconstruction |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supervised Learning** powers the perception stack in autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), medical diagnostic AIs (skin cancer detection, diabetic retinopathy), and predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0.
- **Unsupervised Learning** is essential for IoT anomaly detection in smart cities, recommendation engines (Netflix, Spotify), and unsupervised pre-training of large language models.
- **Reinforcement Learning** is revolutionizing robotics (Boston Dynamics), game AI (DeepMind's AlphaGo), chip design (Google's RL for chip floorplanning), and energy-efficient HVAC control in buildings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Perceptron Update Rule (Supervised Learning Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** The Perceptron is the *simplest supervised learning model* and its derivation is a 14-mark favorite in KTU exams. We will derive the update rule from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Model Definition

The Perceptron takes input vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, computes a weighted sum, applies a step activation, and outputs:

$$\hat{y} = \phi\!\left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j x_j + b \right) = \phi(w^{\top} x + b)$$

where $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the weight vector, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias, and $\phi$ is the **Heaviside step function**:

$$\phi(z) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } z \ge \theta \\ -1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\theta$ is the threshold (often absorbed into bias by setting $\theta = 0$ and $x_0 = 1$).

### 3.1.2 Loss / Error Definition

The Perceptron learning rule is based on minimizing the *perceptron criterion* (a surrogate loss):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{perc}}(w) = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} y^{(i)} (w^{\top} x^{(i)})$$

where $\mathcal{M} = \{ i : y^{(i)}(w^{\top} x^{(i)}) \le 0 \}$ is the set of **misclassified samples**.

> **Intuition:** We only penalize misclassified points. If $y^{(i)} = +1$ but $w^{\top}x^{(i)} < 0$ (i.e., model says negative for a positive sample), the product is negative, and the negative sign in front makes the loss positive. Correctly classified points have a positive product and contribute zero to the loss.

### 3.1.3 Gradient Computation

We compute the gradient of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{perc}}$ with respect to $w$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{perc}}}{\partial w} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} y^{(i)} x^{(i)}$$

**Reasoning:** The derivative of $y^{(i)} w^{\top} x^{(i)}$ with respect to $w$ is $y^{(i)} x^{(i)}$ (treating $y^{(i)}$ and $x^{(i)}$ as constants). The negative sign carries through.

### 3.1.4 Applying Gradient Descent

With learning rate $\eta > 0$, the update rule is:

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{perc}}}{\partial w} = w + \eta \sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} y^{(i)} x^{(i)}$$

For **stochastic (online) Perceptron** — updating after each sample:

$$\boxed{\,w \leftarrow w + \eta \, y^{(i)} x^{(i)}\,}$$

with the bias updating as $b \leftarrow b + \eta \, y^{(i)}$.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $w = (1, -1)^{\top}$, $b = 0$, $\eta = 1$, with training sample $x = (1, 1)^{\top}$, $y = +1$.

- Compute the pre-activation: $z = w^{\top} x + b = (1)(1) + (-1)(1) + 0 = 0$.
- Apply step function: $\hat{y} = \phi(0) = +1$. **Correctly classified** (since $y = +1$).
- No update needed: $w$ stays at $(1, -1)^{\top}$.

Now consider a second sample: $x = (0, 1)^{\top}$, $y = -1$.

- Compute: $z = (1)(0) + (-1)(1) + 0 = -1$.
- Prediction: $\hat{y} = \phi(-1) = -1$. **Correctly classified**. No update.

Third sample: $x = (1, 0)^{\top}$, $y = -1$.

- Compute: $z = (1)(1) + (-1)(0) = 1$.
- Prediction: $\hat{y} = \phi(1) = +1$. **Misclassified** (true label is $-1$).
- Apply update: $w_{\text{new}} = w + \eta \, y \, x = (1, -1)^{\top} + (1)(-1)(1, 0)^{\top} = (0, -1)^{\top}$.
- $b_{\text{new}} = 0 + (1)(-1) = -1$.

The Perceptron has rotated the decision boundary to better classify this point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Bellman Equation (Reinforcement Learning Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** The Bellman equation is the cornerstone of nearly all RL algorithms. KTU expects you to derive it from the definition of the return.

### 3.2.1 Starting from the Return

By definition:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} \, r_{t+3} + \dots = r_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+2}$$

Observe that the sum on the right is precisely $G_{t+1}$:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

### 3.2.2 Taking the Expectation (State-Value Function)

The state-value function is $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t = s]$. Applying expectation to both sides of the recursive return:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right]$$

Splitting the expectation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right]$$

### 3.2.3 Expanding the First Expectation (Law of Total Expectation)

We condition on the action and the next state:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ r_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

**Reasoning:** First pick action $a$ with probability $\pi(a \vert s)$; then environment transitions to $s'$ with probability $P(s' \vert s, a)$, producing reward $R(s, a, s')$.

### 3.2.4 Expanding the Second Expectation

By the Markov property, given $s_{t+1} = s'$, the future depends only on $s'$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ G_{t+1} \,\big\vert\, s_t = s \right] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$$

### 3.2.5 Combining the Two Terms

Substituting back:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This is the **Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2.6 Worked Numerical Example — A 3-State MDP

Consider a deterministic MDP with states $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ and two actions $\{a, b\}$. Transition and reward tables:

| From $s$ | Action $a$ | To $s'$ | Reward |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $a$ | $s_2$ | $+5$ |
| $s_1$ | $b$ | $s_3$ | $-2$ |
| $s_2$ | $a$ | $s_3$ | $+3$ |
| $s_2$ | $b$ | $s_2$ | $0$ |
| $s_3$ | $a$ | $s_3$ | $0$ (terminal) |
| $s_3$ | $b$ | $s_3$ | $0$ (terminal) |

Assume $\gamma = 0.9$ and a uniform policy $\pi(a \vert s) = \pi(b \vert s) = 0.5$.

**Compute $V^{\pi}(s_3)$:** $s_3$ is absorbing with zero future reward, so $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$.

**Compute $V^{\pi}(s_2)$:**

$$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 0.5 \cdot [3 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_3)] + 0.5 \cdot [0 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_2)]$$
$$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 0.5 \cdot [3 + 0] + 0.5 \cdot [0 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_2)]$$
$$V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1.5 + 0.45 \, V^{\pi}(s_2)$$
$$0.55 \, V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1.5 \implies V^{\pi}(s_2) = 1.5 / 0.55 \approx 2.727$$

**Compute $V^{\pi}(s_1)$:**

$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot [5 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_2)] + 0.5 \cdot [-2 + 0.9 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_3)]$$
$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot [5 + 0.9 \cdot 2.727] + 0.5 \cdot [-2 + 0]$$
$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot [5 + 2.454] + 0.5 \cdot (-2) = 0.5 \cdot 7.454 - 1 = 3.727 - 1 = 2.727$$

So $V^{\pi}(s_1) \approx 2.727$, $V^{\pi}(s_2) \approx 2.727$, $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$.

> **Interpretation:** Under the uniform policy, $s_1$ and $s_2$ are equally valuable; the agent collects comparable cumulative reward starting from either.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The K-Means Update Rule (Unsupervised Learning Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** K-Means is the most widely-used clustering algorithm. The KTU board tests derivation of the centroid update.

### 3.3.1 Objective Recap

Minimize $J = \sum_{i}\sum_{k} r_{ik}\vert\vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{k}\vert\vert^{2}$.

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Fix Centroids, Optimize Assignments

For a given $\mu_{k}$, to minimize $J$, assign each $x^{(i)}$ to its nearest centroid:

$$r_{ik} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k = \arg\min_{j} \vert\vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{j}\vert\vert^{2} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Reasoning:** Each $x^{(i)}$ appears in exactly one term of the inner sum; pick the term with smallest squared distance to set $r_{ik} = 1$ and the rest to 0.

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Fix Assignments, Optimize Centroids

For fixed $r_{ik}$, take the partial derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_{k}$ and set to 0:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_{k}} = -2 \sum_{i} r_{ik} (x^{(i)} - \mu_{k}) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{i} r_{ik} \, x^{(i)} = \mu_{k} \sum_{i} r_{ik}$$

$$\mu_{k} = \frac{\sum_{i} r_{ik} \, x^{(i)}}{\sum_{i} r_{ik}}$$

This is the **mean of all points assigned to cluster $k$** — hence the name "K-Means". $\blacksquare$

### 3.3.4 Worked Numerical Example

Points: $x^{(1)} = (1, 1)$, $x^{(2)} = (1, 2)$, $x^{(3)} = (5, 4)$, $x^{(4)} = (5, 5)$. $K = 2$.

**Initialize** $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$, $\mu_2 = (5, 4)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**
- $x^{(1)} = (1,1)$: distance to $\mu_1 = 0$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{16+9}=5$ → assign to cluster 1.
- $x^{(2)} = (1,2)$: distance to $\mu_1 = 1$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{16+4} \approx 4.47$ → assign to cluster 1.
- $x^{(3)} = (5,4)$: distance to $\mu_1 = 4$, to $\mu_2 = 0$ → assign to cluster 2.
- $x^{(4)} = (5,5)$: distance to $\mu_1 \approx 5.66$, to $\mu_2 = 1$ → assign to cluster 2.

**Update Centroids:**
- $\mu_1 = \frac{(1,1) + (1,2)}{2} = (1, 1.5)$.
- $\mu_2 = \frac{(5,4) + (5,5)}{2} = (5, 4.5)$.

**Iteration 2:** Assignments remain unchanged. **Convergence reached**.

Final clusters: $C_1 = \{(1,1), (1,2)\}$ with centroid $(1, 1.5)$, and $C_2 = \{(5,4), (5,5)\}$ with centroid $(5, 4.5)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Paradigm Comparison

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data requirement** | Large labeled dataset | Unlabeled dataset | Interactive environment (simulator) |
| **Feedback signal** | Per-sample label | None (self-supervised) | Scalar, delayed reward |
| **Optimization target** | $\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(y, f_{\theta}(x))]$ | $\min_{\theta} \, D(p_{\text{data}}, p_{\theta})$ or $J_{\text{clustering}}$ | $\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t]$ |
| **Evaluation metric** | Accuracy, F1, MSE | Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, reconstruction error | Cumulative reward, regret |
| **Convergence guarantee** | Yes (for convex loss); no (for non-convex NN) | Yes (K-Means → local min) | Yes for tabular; no for deep RL |
| **Key challenge** | Labeling cost, overfitting | Choosing $K$ / dimensions | Exploration–exploitation |
| **Sample efficiency** | High (uses labels efficiently) | High (no labels needed) | Low (often needs millions of episodes) |
| **Stability** | High (with sufficient data) | Moderate | Often unstable |
| **KTU exam weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supervised Learning Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Data X] --> B[Neural Network Model]
    B --> C[Predicted Output Y_hat]
    D[True Label Y] --> E[Loss Function L]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Compute Gradients]
    F --> G[Optimizer]
    G --> H[Update Weights W]
    H --> B
    I[New Epoch] --> A
```

**Description:** The model generates predictions; the loss function measures the discrepancy between predicted and true labels; backpropagation computes gradients; the optimizer (SGD, Adam) updates weights. This loop continues for many epochs.

---

## 4.2 Unsupervised Learning Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unlabeled Data X] --> B[Feature Extractor / Encoder]
    B --> C[Latent Representation Z]
    C --> D[Cluster Assignment / Decoder]
    D --> E[Reconstruction X_hat OR Cluster Labels]
    F[Reconstruction Loss] --> G[Update Encoder/Decoder]
    E --> F
```

**Description:** The encoder compresses input into a latent code $z$. Either $z$ is used directly for clustering/dimensionality reduction, or a decoder reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$, and the reconstruction loss trains both networks.

---

## 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent Policy pi] -->|Action a_t| B[Environment]
    B -->|State s_t+1| A
    B -->|Reward r_t+1| A
    C[Experience Buffer] --> A
    A --> D[Value Function V or Q]
    D --> A
```

**Description:** The agent observes state $s_t$, selects action $a_t \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s_t)$, the environment transitions to $s_{t+1}$ and emits reward $r_{t+1}$. The agent stores the experience tuple $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ and uses it to update its policy and value function.

---

## 4.4 Unified Three-Paradigm Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Learning Paradigm Selection]) --> SL{Is Data Labeled?}
    SL -- Yes --> Sup[Supervised Learning]
    SL -- No --> SU{Is there an Interactive Environment?}
    SU -- No --> Unsup[Unsupervised Learning]
    SU -- Yes --> RL[Reinforcement Learning]
    Sup --> SupAlgo[Perceptron, MLP, CNN, RNN, SVM]
    Unsup --> UnsupAlgo[K-Means, PCA, Autoencoder, SOM, GAN]
    RL --> RLAlgo[Q-Learning, DQN, PPO, Actor-Critic]
    SupAlgo --> Out1[Trained Predictor]
    UnsupAlgo --> Out2[Cluster Model / Generator]
    RLAlgo --> Out3[Optimal Policy]
```

**Description:** A decision-tree-style flowchart that a student can use to choose the right learning paradigm for a given problem.

---

## 4.5 The Perceptron Geometric Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Space] --> B[Linear Decision Boundary w.x + b = 0]
    B --> C[Class +1 Region]
    B --> D[Class -1 Region]
    E[Misclassified Point] -.->|Update w| B
```

**Description:** The Perceptron separates two classes using a hyperplane $w^{\top}x + b = 0$. Misclassified points (lying on the wrong side) trigger weight updates that rotate the hyperplane toward correct classification.

---

## 4.6 The Bellman Backup Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[State s] -->|Action a| S1[Next State s']
    S -->|Reward| R[Reward r]
    S1 --> V1[Value V prime]
    V1 -->|Discount gamma| B[Bellman Backup]
    R --> B
    S --> V[Value V of s]
    B --> V
```

**Description:** Illustrates how the value of state $s$ is computed by combining the immediate reward $r$ with the discounted value of the successor state $V^{\pi}(s')$. This is the *backup* operation at the heart of dynamic programming and RL.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define supervised learning. Give two examples of supervised learning tasks and name two algorithms used.**

**Model Answer:**
Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which the model is trained on a dataset consisting of input-output pairs $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}$. The model learns a mapping $f_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that minimizes the discrepancy between its predictions $\hat{y}$ and the true labels $y$.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Image classification** (e.g., classifying X-ray images as normal/abnormal).
2. **House price prediction** (regression, predicting continuous prices).

**Two Algorithms:** Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with Backpropagation, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, k-Nearest Neighbors (any two).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is reinforcement learning? Differentiate it from supervised learning.**

**Model Answer:**
**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a learning paradigm where an agent learns an optimal policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ by interacting with an environment. At each time step, the agent observes a state, takes an action, and receives a scalar reward. The goal is to maximize the expected cumulative discounted return $G_t = \sum \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$.

| Aspect | Supervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Correct label for each input | Scalar reward (delayed) |
| Data | Static labeled dataset | Sequential, interactive |
| Goal | Minimize prediction error | Maximize cumulative reward |
| Examples | Image classification | Game playing (Chess) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the supervised learning paradigm in detail. Discuss the role of the loss function and describe the backpropagation algorithm with suitable equations.

**Model Answer:**

**Supervised Learning — Detailed Explanation:**

In supervised learning, the network is given $N$ labeled training examples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The goal is to learn parameters $\theta$ that produce a function $f_{\theta}(x)$ such that $f_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) \approx y^{(i)}$ for all $i$, while generalizing well to unseen data.

**Role of the Loss Function:**
The loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ quantifies the error between the predicted output $\hat{y} = f_{\theta}(x)$ and the true label $y$. Common loss functions include:
- Mean Squared Error (regression): $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^{2}$
- Cross-Entropy (classification): $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}\sum_{c} y_{c}^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_{c}^{(i)}$

The loss function serves as the optimization objective. By minimizing $\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(y^{(i)}, f_{\theta}(x^{(i)}))$ using gradient-based methods, the network parameters are iteratively refined.

**Backpropagation Algorithm:**

Backpropagation is the algorithm used to efficiently compute the gradient $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ in a multi-layer neural network. It applies the chain rule of calculus layer-by-layer backward from the output to the input.

For a network with $L$ layers, the steps are:

1. **Forward Pass:** Compute activations $a^{(l)}$ at each layer $l$:
$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}, \quad a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$$

2. **Compute Output Error:** The error at the output layer:
$$\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\hat{y}}\mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

3. **Backward Pass:** Propagate the error to earlier layers:
$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

4. **Compute Gradients:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \, a^{(l-1)\top}, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

5. **Update Weights:** Using gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$:
$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

The chain rule allows gradients at any layer to be computed as a product of local Jacobians, making the algorithm computationally efficient — $\mathcal{O}(\text{weights})$ per sample.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a multi-layer perceptron (MLP). Show the mathematical formulation of forward propagation for an $L$-layer network with sigmoid activation.

**Model Answer:**

**MLP Architecture:**

An MLP is a feedforward neural network consisting of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Each layer is fully connected to the next.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
Input Layer        Hidden Layer 1     Hidden Layer 2     Output Layer
x1 --o--           ---o---           ---o---             ---o--- y1
x2 --o--o--o--     o-o-o-o-          -o-o-o-o-           ---o--- y2
x3 --o--           ---o---           ---o---
x4 --o--           ---o---           ---o---
                    (h1=5)            (h2=3)              (output=2)
```

Each circle represents a neuron. Every neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Let $a^{(0)} = x$ be the input. For each layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$:

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

$$a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(l)}}}$$

where:
- $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l} \times n_{l-1}}$ is the weight matrix of layer $l$
- $b^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l}}$ is the bias vector
- $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
- $a^{(l)}$ is the activation (output) of layer $l$

The final output is $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$.

**For a 3-layer MLP (1 hidden layer + 1 output layer, $L = 2$):**

- Layer 1 (hidden): $a^{(1)} = \sigma(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)})$
- Layer 2 (output): $\hat{y} = a^{(2)} = \sigma(W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)})$

The total parameter count is $n_0 \cdot n_1 + n_1 + n_1 \cdot n_2 + n_2$ where $n_l$ is the number of neurons in layer $l$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain unsupervised learning. Discuss K-Means clustering algorithm with its objective function. Perform K-Means clustering by hand for the following 2D points: $A = (1, 1)$, $B = (2, 1)$, $C = (4, 3)$, $D = (5, 4)$, with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$, $\mu_2 = (5, 4)$. Show two iterations.

**Model Answer:**

**Unsupervised Learning — Detailed Explanation:**

Unsupervised learning deals with datasets that have *no* labeled outputs. The model must discover hidden structure in the input distribution $p(x)$ on its own. Common tasks include:
- **Clustering** (grouping similar data)
- **Dimensionality Reduction** (projecting to lower dimensions)
- **Density Estimation** (modeling $p(x)$)
- **Generative Modeling** (sampling new data from $p(x)$)

**K-Means Algorithm:**

K-Means partitions $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (WCSS):

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} \,\left\| x^{(i)} - \mu_{k} \right\|_{2}^{2}$$

where $r_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether $x^{(i)}$ is in cluster $k$, and $\mu_{k}$ is the centroid of cluster $k$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $K$ centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_K$ (e.g., randomly).
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each point to the nearest centroid.
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until convergence.

**Numerical Computation:**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**
- $A = (1, 1)$: distance to $\mu_1 = 0$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{16+9} = 5$. Assign to $C_1$.
- $B = (2, 1)$: distance to $\mu_1 = 1$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$. Assign to $C_1$.
- $C = (4, 3)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$. Assign to $C_2$.
- $D = (5, 4)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{16+9} = 5$, to $\mu_2 = 0$. Assign to $C_2$.

**Iteration 1 — Update:**
- $\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{(1,1) + (2,1)}{2} = (1.5, 1.0)$
- $\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{(4,3) + (5,4)}{2} = (4.5, 3.5)$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment:**
- $A = (1, 1)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{0.25 + 0} = 0.5$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{12.25 + 6.25} = \sqrt{18.5} \approx 4.30$. Assign to $C_1$.
- $B = (2, 1)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{0.25 + 0} = 0.5$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{6.25 + 6.25} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.54$. Assign to $C_1$.
- $C = (4, 3)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{6.25 + 4} = \sqrt{10.25} \approx 3.20$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.71$. Assign to $C_2$.
- $D = (5, 4)$: distance to $\mu_1 = \sqrt{12.25 + 9} = \sqrt{21.25} \approx 4.61$, to $\mu_2 = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} \approx 0.71$. Assign to $C_2$.

**Iteration 2 — Update:**
- $\mu_1 = (1.5, 1.0)$ (unchanged).
- $\mu_2 = (4.5, 3.5)$ (unchanged).

**Convergence reached.** Final clusters: $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ with centroid $(1.5, 1.0)$, $C_2 = \{C, D\}$ with centroid $(4.5, 3.5)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the reinforcement learning paradigm. Derive the Bellman equation for the state-value function from the definition of the return.

**Model Answer:**

**Reinforcement Learning Paradigm:**

Reinforcement Learning is a goal-directed learning paradigm where an **agent** interacts with a dynamic **environment** to learn an optimal **policy**. At each time step $t$:
1. The agent observes the current state $s_t$.
2. It selects an action $a_t$ based on its policy $\pi(a_t \vert s_t)$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \vert s_t, a_t)$.
4. The agent receives a reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.

The objective is to find a policy $\pi$ that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted return:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1}$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the discount factor. RL is appropriate for sequential decision-making problems such as game playing, robotics, and autonomous control.

**Derivation of the Bellman Equation:**

**Step 1: Recursive Decomposition of the Return.**

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} \, r_{t+3} + \dots$$
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \left( r_{t+2} + \gamma \, r_{t+3} + \dots \right)$$
$$\boxed{G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}}$$

**Step 2: Take Expectation Given $s_t = s$.**

The state-value function is $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t = s]$. By linearity of expectation:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[r_{t+1} \vert s_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \vert s_t = s]$$

**Step 3: Apply the Law of Total Expectation (over action $a$ and next state $s'$).**

For the immediate reward:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[r_{t+1} \vert s_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \, R(s, a, s')$$

For the future return, using the Markov property (future depends only on $s'$):
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \vert s_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 4: Combine.**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This is the **Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$ (policy evaluation) with the Bellman *optimality* equation for $V^{*}$ (optimal control). The optimality equation has a $\max_{a}$ outside the sum, not $\pi(a \vert s)$.
> - **Always** state the Markov property explicitly when deriving the Bellman equation — forgetting it costs 1–2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, do NOT skip the iteration table; KTU examiners award partial credit for each correctly shown step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Learning Methods**

### Supervised Learning
- ✅ Requires labeled dataset $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}$
- ✅ Loss functions: MSE (regression), Cross-Entropy (classification)
- ✅ Gradient descent update: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$
- ✅ Backpropagation uses chain rule to compute gradients efficiently
- ✅ Examples: Perceptron, MLP, CNN, SVM

### Unsupervised Learning
- ✅ No labels — discovers hidden structure
- ✅ K-Means minimizes WCSS: $J = \sum r_{ik}\vert\vert x^{(i)} - \mu_{k}\vert\vert^{2}$
- ✅ PCA finds eigenvectors of covariance matrix (maximize variance)
- ✅ Autoencoders learn compressed latent representations
- ✅ Examples: K-Means, PCA, Autoencoders, SOM, GAN

### Reinforcement Learning
- ✅ Agent–Environment loop: state → action → reward → new state
- ✅ Return: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$
- ✅ State-value: $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \vert s_t = s]$
- ✅ Bellman equation: $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$
- ✅ Bellman optimality: $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$
- ✅ Q-Learning update: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$
- ✅ Examples: Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, PPO, Actor-Critic

### Cross-Paradigm Insights
- ✅ Supervised needs labels → expensive in real engineering
- ✅ Unsupervised finds structure but hard to evaluate
- ✅ RL handles sequential decisions but has exploration–exploitation dilemma
- ✅ Hybrid approaches (e.g., self-supervised pre-training) bridge paradigms

### Key Formulas to Memorize (Top 5 for KTU)
1. **Gradient Descent:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$
2. **MSE Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(y - \hat{y})^{2}$
3. **Return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$
4. **Bellman Equation:** $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a}\pi(a\vert s)\sum_{s'}P(s'\vert s,a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$
5. **K-Means Update:** $\mu_{k} = \frac{\sum_{i} r_{ik} x^{(i)}}{\sum_{i} r_{ik}}$

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Learning Methods**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taxonomy of Neural Network Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT522 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Taxonomy of Neural Network Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Taxonomy of Neural Network Architectures** is the systematic, hierarchical classification of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models based on their **structural topology**, **information flow direction**, **connectivity patterns**, **depth of layers**, **learning paradigm**, and **functional specialization**. It provides a unified engineering framework for categorizing networks such as Feedforward Networks, Recurrent Networks, Convolutional Networks, Modular/Hybrid Networks, and Self-Organizing Networks as per the KTU 2024 Scheme ANN syllabus.

In essence, the taxonomy acts as a **decision matrix** that allows an engineer to map a given problem (regression, classification, sequence prediction, generative modelling, clustering) to the most suitable neural network class.

### 1.2 Key Classification Criteria

The KTU 2024 ANN module classifies neural networks along **six orthogonal axes**:

1. **Network Topology (Architecture)** – How neurons are physically arranged and connected.
2. **Information Flow Direction** – Whether signals move strictly forward or also loop back.
3. **Learning Paradigm** – How the network acquires knowledge (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, hybrid).
4. **Connectivity Density** – Whether every neuron connects to every other (fully connected) or selectively (locally/convolutionally).
5. **Network Depth** – Number of hidden layers (shallow vs. deep).
6. **Functional Specialization** – The dominant mathematical operation (dot product, convolution, radial basis, competitive learning, generative sampling).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Vehicle Classification System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of neural network architectures the way automobile engineers classify vehicles. A car is not just "a car" — it is a *Sedan*, *SUV*, *Truck*, or *Sports Car*, each with a specific chassis, drivetrain (FWD/RWD/AWD), fuel type (Petrol/Diesel/Electric), and purpose (commute/off-road/racing). Likewise, neural networks are not just "deep learning" — they are *feedforward*, *recurrent*, *convolutional*, or *generative*, each with a specific data flow, weight-sharing scheme, learning rule, and application domain.

When a KTU examiner asks "classify the network," the expected answer is essentially a **multi-dimensional sorting** — placing the model in the correct row (paradigm) and column (topology) of the taxonomy matrix.

### 1.4 Physical / Numerical Standards Highlighted in Bold

- **Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT):** A feedforward network with **one hidden layer** containing a **finite** number of neurons can approximate **any continuous function** on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$, under mild assumptions on the activation function.
- **Universal Depth Efficiency:** A deep network of $L$ layers can represent certain functions **exponentially more compactly** than a shallow network — this is the **depth efficiency** principle.
- **Standard depth threshold:** Networks with $\geq 2$ hidden layers are conventionally termed **Deep Neural Networks (DNNs)** in the KTU 2024 scheme.
- **Vanishing gradient threshold (empirical):** Gradient norm $< 10^{-7}$ typically signals vanishing gradients in deep sigmoid-based networks.

### 1.5 The Five Major Architectural Families (KTU 2024 Module 1 Coverage)

| # | Family | One-Line Essence |
|---|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Single-Layer Perceptron (SLP)** | Linear threshold unit; only direct input→output mapping. |
| 2 | **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** | Stack of fully-connected layers with non-linear activations. |
| 3 | **Feedback / Recurrent Networks (RNN, Hopfield, Boltzmann)** | Contains feedback loops enabling memory of past states. |
| 4 | **Self-Organizing Networks (SOM, ART, RBF)** | Competitive / radial-basis learning for clustering. |
| 5 | **Modular & Hybrid Architectures (CNN, LSTM, GAN, Autoencoders)** | Specialized modules combined for complex tasks. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always introduce the taxonomy with the **"Two Master Classes"** — **Feedforward (acyclic, no memory)** versus **Feedback/Recurrent (cyclic, dynamic memory)** — and then drill down into sub-categories. This is the single most rewarded structure in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The taxonomy is constructed by sequentially answering **six binary/ternary questions** about any given neural network:

1. **Does the network have cycles in its connection graph?**
   - **No** → **Feedforward Architecture** (acyclic DAG).
   - **Yes** → **Recurrent / Feedback Architecture** (cyclic graph).

2. **How many trainable layers exist?**
   - **1 layer** (input directly to output) → **Single-Layer Perceptron (SLP)**.
   - **≥ 2 hidden layers** → **Multi-Layer / Deep Network**.

3. **What is the connectivity density?**
   - **Every neuron to every neuron** → **Fully Connected (Dense)**.
   - **Local receptive fields, shared weights** → **Convolutional / Locally Connected**.

4. **What is the learning signal?**
   - **Labeled targets** → **Supervised**.
   - **No labels, structure discovery** → **Unsupervised**.
   - **Scalar reward from environment** → **Reinforcement**.
   - **Generator–Discriminator duel** → **Adversarial**.

5. **What is the dominant neuron model?**
   - **Dot product + sigmoid/tanh/ReLU** → **McCulloch–Pitts / Perceptron family**.
   - **Radial basis (Gaussian distance)** → **RBF Network**.
   - **Winner-takes-all competition** → **SOM / LVQ**.

6. **What is the primary application?**
   - **Sequence data** → **RNN / LSTM / GRU**.
   - **Image / spatial grids** → **CNN**.
   - **Generative / sampling** → **GAN / VAE / Boltzmann**.
   - **Dimensionality reduction** → **Autoencoder**.

### 2.2 Why Each Architecture Exists — Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No architecture is "best" universally.** Each is a tailored trade-off among three engineering constraints: (1) **Expressive Power**, (2) **Training Tractability (gradient flow)**, (3) **Inductive Bias (assumptions about data)**.

- **MLP** — universal approximator, weak inductive bias → needs huge data.
- **CNN** — strong spatial locality bias → excellent for images, parameter-efficient via weight sharing.
- **RNN/LSTM** — strong temporal dependency bias → excellent for sequences.
- **SOM** — preserves topological structure of input → excellent for visualization/clustering.
- **Hopfield** — associative memory → pattern completion.
- **GAN** — implicit density estimation → realistic sample generation.
- **Autoencoder** — bottleneck forces compressed latent representation → denoising, dimensionality reduction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Markdown tables break when `|` appears in formulas. All absolute values and set delimiters use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Neuron Pre-activation | $u_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij}\, x_j + b_i$ | Scalar, weighted sum of inputs |
| 2 | Neuron Output (Activation) | $y_i = \varphi(u_i)$ | Output of activation $\varphi$ |
| 3 | Layer Parameter Count | $P_{\text{layer}} = (n_{\text{in}} \times n_{\text{out}}) + n_{\text{out}}$ | Weights + biases (unitless integer) |
| 4 | Total Network Parameters | $P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} (n_{l-1}\, n_{l} + n_{l})$ | Sum over all $L$ layers |
| 5 | Universal Approximation Bound | $\vert f(x) - \hat{f}(x) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ for some $N$ hidden units | $\varepsilon > 0$ arbitrary |
| 6 | RNN Hidden State Update | $h_t = \varphi_h(W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\, x_t + b_h)$ | Recurrence relation |
| 7 | CNN Convolution Operation | $y_{i,j} = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} K_{m,n}\, x_{i+m,\, j+n}$ | Discrete 2D convolution |
| 8 | Receptive Field (CNN) | $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$ | Pixels covered at layer $l$ |
| 9 | RBF Activation | $\varphi(u) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert u - c \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | Gaussian radial basis |
| 10 | SOM Neighborhood Function | $h_{ci}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert r_c - r_i \vert^{2}}{2\sigma(t)^{2}}\right)$ | Decays over training time $t$ |
| 11 | Hopfield Energy Function | $E = -\dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_{ij}\, s_i\, s_j$ | Lyapunov energy (decreases monotonically) |
| 12 | Boltzmann Probability | $P(s) = \dfrac{\exp(-E(s)/T)}{Z}$ | $Z$ = partition function, $T$ = temperature |
| 13 | GAN Objective (Minimax) | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Value function $V$ |
| 14 | Autoencoder Reconstruction | $\mathcal{L} = \vert\vert x - \hat{x} \vert\vert^{2}$ | Mean Squared Reconstruction Error |
| 15 | Depth Efficiency (informal) | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ layers can replace $\mathcal{O}(N)$ shallow units | $N$ = function complexity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Architecture | Real-World Application |
|--------------|------------------------|
| MLP | Credit scoring, medical diagnosis, tabular regression |
| CNN | Facial recognition, medical imaging, autonomous driving vision |
| RNN/LSTM | Speech recognition, machine translation, stock prediction |
| SOM | Customer segmentation, exploratory data analysis (EDA) |
| RBF | Function interpolation, control system identification |
| Hopfield | Pattern completion, combinatorial optimization (associative memory) |
| GAN | Synthetic data augmentation, super-resolution, deepfakes (defensive use) |
| Autoencoder | Anomaly detection, denoising, dimensionality reduction (PCA generalization) |
